blocks.name: General and Miscellaneous Mathematical Topics
blocks.description: Covers material that sits outside the standard disciplinary classification: general and recreational mathematics, mathematics education and popularization, philosophy and methodology of mathematics, mathematical modeling and simulation, dictionaries and reference works, and the interfaces of mathematics with physics, music, art, architecture, sport, and society. This subclass functions as the MSC scheme's catch-all for cross-cutting, pedagogical, and interdisciplinary material that has no natural home in a specialized discipline. It is administrative and expository in character rather than a site of original research.

brief.name: General and miscellaneous specific topics
brief.definition: Groups cross-disciplinary and non-technical mathematical material — general/recreational mathematics, education, popularization, philosophy and methodology, modeling and simulation, reference works, and mathematics' interfaces with physics, the arts, and society — that does not belong to any single specialized MSC area.

blocks.name: Collections, Proceedings, and Collected Works
blocks.description: Classifies published volumes by their editorial format rather than by mathematical content: conference proceedings, festschriften, collected or selected works of an individual, and multi-author collections of articles. Such classification labels are attached alongside a subject-matter code to indicate that a work is an edited compilation rather than a monograph or a single-author paper. The category is purely bibliographic and administrative, reflecting how mathematical literature is packaged and archived rather than any body of mathematical theory.

brief.name: Conference proceedings and collections of articles
brief.definition: Bibliographic classification for edited volumes — conference proceedings, festschriften, and collected or selected works — used to tag a publication's editorial format independent of its mathematical subject matter.

blocks.name: History of Mathematics and Mathematicians
blocks.description: Studies the historical development of mathematical ideas, methods, and notation across cultures and eras, alongside the biographies, correspondence, and institutional contexts of mathematicians. Draws on primary-source philology, archival research, and the history of science to reconstruct how concepts such as number, proof, and infinity evolved. Encompasses both broad surveys of mathematical civilizations and focused studies of individual theorems, texts, or figures, informing how the discipline understands its own conceptual lineage.

brief.name: History of mathematics and mathematicians
brief.definition: Studies the origins, evolution, transmission, and biographical/institutional context of mathematical ideas, texts, and practitioners across all periods and cultures.

blocks.name: Deductive Systems, Proof, and Foundations
blocks.description: Studies the syntactic side of logic: the formal languages, inference systems, and philosophical frameworks used to formalize mathematical reasoning, together with the algebraic structures that encode them. Encompasses propositional, predicate, modal, and intuitionistic calculi, the analysis of formal proofs as combinatorial objects (ordinal analysis, cut-elimination), constructive mathematics, and the translation of logical systems into algebras such as Boolean, Heyting, and cylindric algebras. Foundational and philosophical questions about what counts as a proof or a foundation for mathematics anchor the area.

brief.name: Philosophical aspects of logic and foundations
brief.definition: Examines the philosophical, historical, and methodological questions surrounding logical systems and the foundations of mathematics.

brief.name: General logic
brief.definition: Covers classical and non-classical propositional and predicate calculi — including many-valued, modal, intuitionistic, and other logics — and their general model-theoretic and proof-theoretic properties.

brief.name: Proof theory and constructive mathematics
brief.definition: Analyzes formal proofs as mathematical objects, measuring the strength of theories via ordinal analysis and cut-elimination, and develops constructive alternatives to classical mathematics.

brief.name: Algebraic logic
brief.definition: Studies algebraic structures — Boolean algebras, Heyting algebras, cylindric and relation algebras — that model logical systems and recast logical questions as algebraic ones.

blocks.name: Model Theory, Set Theory, and Computability
blocks.description: Studies the semantic and structural side of logic: the interpretations, structures, and universes in which formal theories are realized, and the limits of algorithmic definability within them. Model theory classifies structures by the sentences they satisfy; set theory develops the cumulative universe of sets via cardinal and ordinal arithmetic, forcing, and large cardinals; computability theory classifies functions and sets by their algorithmic complexity; nonstandard models exploit ultraproduct constructions to build exotic models of arithmetic, analysis, and set theory. These areas sit at the active research frontier of contemporary logic, driving deep interactions with algebra, analysis, and combinatorics.

brief.name: Model theory
brief.definition: Studies the correspondence between formal languages and the structures that interpret them, classifying structures by the sentences they satisfy.

brief.name: Computability and recursion theory
brief.definition: Studies which functions and sets are algorithmically computable or definable, and organizes the resulting hierarchy of unsolvability degrees.

brief.name: Set theory
brief.definition: Develops the foundational theory of sets, including cardinal and ordinal arithmetic, the axiom of choice, forcing, and large cardinal axioms.

brief.name: Nonstandard models
brief.definition: Studies nonstandard models of arithmetic, analysis, and set theory built via ultraproducts and related constructions, underlying nonstandard analysis.

blocks.name: Enumerative and Algebraic Combinatorics
blocks.description: Studies exact and asymptotic counting of combinatorial structures — permutations, partitions, trees, lattice paths — via generating functions, bijective proofs, and species theory, alongside the algebraic structures (symmetric functions, representation theory of the symmetric group, posets, matroids, association schemes) that organize and explain these counts. The interplay between counting identities and algebraic invariants (Schur functions, RSK correspondence, Coxeter combinatorics) makes this the theoretical backbone connecting combinatorics to algebra and representation theory. Enumerative techniques are classical and largely systematized, while algebraic combinatorics remains a fertile, actively expanding research front tied to Lie theory and cluster algebras.

brief.name: Enumerative combinatorics
brief.definition: The study of exact and asymptotic counting formulas for combinatorial structures using generating functions, recurrences, and bijective methods.

brief.name: Algebraic combinatorics
brief.definition: The use of algebraic tools — representation theory, symmetric functions, posets, and association schemes — to encode and solve combinatorial problems.

blocks.name: Designs and Configurations
blocks.description: Concerns the existence, construction, and classification of combinatorial designs — block designs, Latin squares, Steiner systems, and finite geometries — built from finite sets with prescribed balance and incidence properties. Design theory underpins coding theory, statistical experiment design, and cryptography, and its central existence questions (such as the completion of design spectra) were resolved only in the last decade using probabilistic and algebraic methods. The field blends deep classical constructions with a still-active frontier around resolvable and highly symmetric designs.

brief.name: Designs and configurations
brief.definition: The theory of finite incidence structures — block designs, Latin squares, and Steiner systems — satisfying prescribed regularity and balance conditions.

blocks.name: Graph Theory and Extremal Combinatorics
blocks.description: Encompasses the structural, algorithmic, and topological study of graphs and hypergraphs alongside extremal questions asking how large or dense a structure can be before it is forced to contain a given substructure or pattern — Turán-type problems, Ramsey theory, and extremal set theory. These two areas share methodology (regularity and container methods, probabilistic and algebraic techniques) and jointly drive much of modern discrete mathematics's connection to computer science and additive combinatorics. Core structural graph theory is mature, but extremal and Ramsey-type questions remain among combinatorics's most active open frontiers, including unresolved Ramsey number bounds and hypergraph Turán problems.

brief.name: Graph theory
brief.definition: The study of graphs and hypergraphs — their structure, coloring, connectivity, and algorithmic properties — as models of pairwise relations.

brief.name: Extremal combinatorics
brief.definition: The study of the maximum or minimum size a combinatorial structure can have while avoiding or guaranteeing a given substructure, including Ramsey and Turán-type problems.

blocks.name: Ordered Sets and Ordered Algebraic Structures
blocks.description: Studies partially ordered sets in their own right — chains, antichains, order-preserving maps, completions, and dimension theory — alongside algebraic structures (groups, rings, fields, semigroups) equipped with a compatible order relation. This block underlies nearly all of order theory, providing the foundational vocabulary (posets, order types, cofinality) on which lattice theory and Boolean algebra are built, and it connects order to algebra via ordered algebraic systems such as ordered groups and ordered fields.

brief.name: Ordered sets
brief.definition: The general theory of partially ordered sets: order relations, chains, antichains, well-founded and well-ordered sets, order-preserving maps, and completions.

brief.name: Ordered structures
brief.definition: Algebraic structures such as groups, rings, semigroups, and fields endowed with a compatible partial or total order, studying how algebraic operations interact with the order relation.

blocks.name: Lattice Theory
blocks.description: Examines lattices — posets in which every pair of elements has a join and meet — and their principal special classes: modular and complemented lattices (generalizing subgroup and subspace lattices), distributive lattices (with their Stone/Priestley duality to topological spaces), and Boolean algebras (the algebraic counterpart of classical propositional logic and set operations). This is the classical algebraic core of order theory, with deep ties to universal algebra, projective geometry, logic, and topology via representation and duality theorems.

brief.name: Lattices
brief.definition: Partially ordered sets in which every pair of elements has a well-defined least upper bound (join) and greatest lower bound (meet), studied as algebraic structures obeying the lattice axioms.

brief.name: Modular lattices, complemented lattices
brief.definition: Lattices satisfying the modular law — generalizing the lattice of subgroups or vector subspaces — or admitting complements for each element, including relatively complemented and orthocomplemented lattices.

brief.name: Distributive lattices
brief.definition: Lattices in which meet and join mutually distribute over one another, a class encompassing Heyting algebras, frames and locales, and studied via Stone and Priestley duality with topological spaces.

brief.name: Boolean algebras (Boolean rings)
brief.definition: Complemented distributive lattices, equivalently rings with idempotent multiplication, that algebraically model the operations of set union, intersection, and complement and underlie classical propositional logic.

blocks.name: Universal Algebra
blocks.description: Universal algebra studies algebraic structures (sets equipped with operations satisfying identities) in full generality, abstracting away from particular signatures to uncover laws common to groups, rings, lattices, and beyond. It develops the theory of varieties (equationally defined classes closed under subalgebras, homomorphic images, and products, per Birkhoff's theorem) and congruence lattices, and extends to non-classical algebra types such as partial algebras, multi-operator algebras, and algebras with infinitary operations. This foundational apparatus underlies model theory's study of algebraic structures and provides the common language for comparing disparate algebraic theories.

brief.name: Algebraic structures
brief.definition: Studies sets endowed with one or more finitary (or infinitary) operations, developing the general theory of homomorphisms, congruences, subalgebras, and direct/subdirect decompositions common to all such structures.

brief.name: Varieties
brief.definition: Investigates equationally axiomatized classes of algebras (varieties in the sense of Birkhoff's HSP theorem), including free algebras, identities, Maltsev conditions, and lattices of subvarieties.

brief.name: Other classes of algebras
brief.definition: Covers algebraic structure classes that fall outside the standard variety framework, such as quasivarieties, partial algebras, and other generalized or non-equational classes of algebras.

blocks.name: Elementary, Combinatorial and Sequence-Based Number Theory
blocks.description: Studies the arithmetic of the integers through elementary, non-analytic tools — divisibility, congruences, and arithmetic functions — together with the combinatorial structure of integer sequences and sets, and the number-theoretic behavior of polynomials and matrices. This is the classical bedrock of the subject, largely settled since the 19th and early 20th centuries, though it continues to seed elementary open problems and underlies every other branch of number theory.

brief.name: Elementary number theory
brief.definition: Studies divisibility, congruences, primality, and arithmetic functions using elementary, non-analytic methods.

brief.name: Sequences and sets
brief.definition: Investigates integer sequences (recurrences, special number sequences) and the additive-combinatorial structure of sets of integers.

brief.name: Polynomials and matrices
brief.definition: Examines number-theoretic properties of polynomials and matrices with integer or arithmetic coefficients, such as discriminants and resultants.

blocks.name: Diophantine Equations, Approximation and Arithmetic Geometry
blocks.description: Concerns integer and rational solutions of polynomial equations (Diophantine equations), their geometric incarnation as points on algebraic varieties over number fields (arithmetic/Diophantine geometry), the lattice-point and convex-body methods of Minkowski used to bound and count solutions (geometry of numbers), and the fine approximation of real and algebraic numbers by rationals, including transcendence proofs. Landmark results — Faltings' theorem and Wiles' proof of Fermat's Last Theorem — sit here, and the area stays intensely active, driven by conjectures such as abc and Vojta's conjecture.

brief.name: Diophantine equations
brief.definition: Studies integer and rational solutions of polynomial equations, including Fermat-type, Pell, and exponential Diophantine equations.

brief.name: Arithmetic algebraic geometry (Diophantine geometry)
brief.definition: Studies rational and integral points on algebraic varieties over number fields, especially elliptic curves and abelian varieties.

brief.name: Geometry of numbers
brief.definition: Applies lattice-point and convex-body geometry, in the spirit of Minkowski, to Diophantine and arithmetic problems.

brief.name: Diophantine approximation, transcendental number theory
brief.definition: Measures how well real and complex numbers are approximated by rationals or algebraics, and proves transcendence and algebraic independence.

blocks.name: Forms, Automorphic Representations and Arithmetic Groups
blocks.description: Studies quadratic and other forms and the linear algebraic groups that act on them, together with automorphic forms and representations attached to discrete arithmetic subgroups of Lie groups. This block is the arithmetic home of the Langlands program, linking Galois representations, modular forms, and L-functions, and it is one of the most intensely researched frontiers in contemporary mathematics.

brief.name: Forms and linear algebraic groups
brief.definition: Studies quadratic and higher-degree forms and the linear algebraic groups that classify and act on them.

brief.name: Discontinuous groups and automorphic forms
brief.definition: Studies modular and automorphic forms on arithmetic discrete subgroups of Lie groups, central to the Langlands program.

blocks.name: Analytic, Multiplicative and Additive Number Theory
blocks.description: Covers the analytic machinery of number theory: equidistribution and metric properties of sequences modulo 1, exponential and character sum estimates, the analytic theory of zeta and L-functions, the distribution of primes, and the additive representation of integers as sums such as in the Goldbach and Waring problems and partition theory. Central open problems — the Riemann Hypothesis, the twin prime conjecture, the Goldbach conjecture — live in this block, making it a major engine of contemporary research.

brief.name: Probabilistic theory: distribution modulo 1; metric theory of algorithms
brief.definition: Studies equidistribution and uniform distribution of sequences modulo 1, and the metric (almost-everywhere) theory of algorithms such as continued fractions.

brief.name: Exponential sums and character sums
brief.definition: Bounds exponential and character sums, tools used throughout number theory to control error terms.

brief.name: Zeta and L-functions: analytic theory
brief.definition: Studies analytic properties — zeros, growth, functional equations — of zeta and L-functions, including the Riemann Hypothesis.

brief.name: Multiplicative number theory
brief.definition: Studies the distribution of primes and the behavior of multiplicative arithmetic functions.

brief.name: Additive number theory; partitions
brief.definition: Studies representations of integers as sums of prescribed types, including Goldbach- and Waring-type problems, and integer partitions.

blocks.name: Algebraic Number Theory and Finite Fields
blocks.description: Develops the arithmetic of number fields and their completions: ideal class groups, unit groups, and Galois theory over global fields, the p-adic and local-field analogues, and number-theoretic properties of finite fields and related commutative rings. Class field theory and local-global principles give this area a mature, largely settled core, even as programs such as Iwasawa theory keep parts of it in active development.

brief.name: Algebraic number theory: global fields
brief.definition: Studies the arithmetic of number fields, including ideal class groups, unit groups, and Galois theory over global fields.

brief.name: Algebraic number theory: local and p-adic fields
brief.definition: Studies local and p-adic fields, their extensions, ramification theory, and p-adic analysis.

brief.name: Finite fields and commutative rings (number-theoretic aspects)
brief.definition: Studies number-theoretic aspects of finite fields and related commutative rings, including polynomial factorization and character sums over them.

blocks.name: Computational, Logical and Applied Number Theory
blocks.description: Gathers the computational, logical, and application-facing corners of number theory: decidability and model-theoretic questions such as Hilbert's tenth problem, algorithms for factorization, primality testing, and other effective computations, and miscellaneous applications of number-theoretic results to fields such as cryptography and coding theory. The computational and applied threads are highly active given cryptographic demand, while the logical connections remain a narrower, more settled specialist niche.

brief.name: Connections of number theory and logic
brief.definition: Studies model-theoretic and decidability questions arising in number theory, such as Hilbert's tenth problem.

brief.name: Computational number theory
brief.definition: Develops and analyzes algorithms for factorization, primality testing, and other explicit number-theoretic computations.

brief.name: Miscellaneous applications of number theory
brief.definition: Covers applications of number-theoretic results outside pure number theory, such as in cryptography and coding theory.

blocks.name: Classical Field Theory and Extensions
blocks.description: Covers the core structural theory of fields: the real and complex fields as prototypical examples, the general axiomatic theory of fields, and the theory of field extensions crowned by Galois theory. Galois cohomology extends these extension-theoretic results into a homological framework, linking field theory to group cohomology and the Brauer group. This block forms the classical, textbook-settled backbone of the discipline, underlying algebraic number theory, algebraic geometry, and Galois representation theory.

brief.name: Real and complex fields
brief.definition: Studies the algebraic, order-theoretic, and analytic structure of the real and complex number fields, real-closed fields, and their formally real generalizations.

brief.name: General field theory
brief.definition: Develops the foundational algebraic theory of fields, including finite fields, polynomial equations over fields, and structural results independent of a particular extension.

brief.name: Field extensions
brief.definition: Studies algebraic, transcendental, separable, and normal field extensions and their Galois groups via the fundamental theorem of Galois theory.

brief.name: Homological methods
brief.definition: Applies cohomological tools, especially Galois cohomology and Brauer group theory, to classify and obstruct field extensions and central simple algebras.

blocks.name: Extended, Topological, and Generalized Field Structures
blocks.description: Groups the subclasses that enrich or relax the field axioms, or place field theory in dialogue with adjacent disciplines. Differential and difference algebra endow fields with extra operators to develop a Galois theory of differential and difference equations; topological fields add a compatible topology, yielding valued and local fields; generalizations of fields loosen commutativity or invertibility axioms (skew fields, near-fields); and the logic connection studies fields through model-theoretic decidability and definability. These areas are actively developed research fronts rather than settled classical theory, each feeding into differential Galois theory, arithmetic geometry, or model theory respectively.

brief.name: Differential and difference algebra
brief.definition: Studies fields and rings equipped with a derivation or difference operator, extending Galois-theoretic methods to differential and difference equations.

brief.name: Topological fields
brief.definition: Studies fields endowed with a compatible topology, including valued fields, local fields, and their completions.

brief.name: Generalizations of fields
brief.definition: Studies near-fields, skew fields, and other algebraic structures obtained by relaxing one or more field axioms.

brief.name: Connections between field theory and logic
brief.definition: Studies the model theory of fields, including quantifier elimination, definability, and decidability of first-order field theories.

blocks.name: Structural Foundations of Commutative Rings
blocks.description: Covers the basic vocabulary of commutative algebra: general ring theory, ring extensions, and local/semilocal rings. These subclasses supply the elementary structural building blocks — ideals, spectra, localization, integral extensions, and local behavior — on which the rest of the discipline is built.

brief.name: General commutative ring theory
brief.definition: Studies the elementary structure of commutative rings — ideals, quotients, localization, and prime spectra — as the foundational vocabulary of commutative algebra.

brief.name: Commutative ring extensions and related topics
brief.definition: Examines extensions of commutative rings, including integral, flat, and faithfully flat extensions, and how ring-theoretic properties behave under base change and descent.

brief.name: Local rings and semilocal rings
brief.definition: Develops the theory of rings with a unique (or finitely many) maximal ideal, including regular local rings and multiplicities, underlying the local structure of algebraic geometry and singularity theory.

blocks.name: Modules, Ideals, and Homological Methods
blocks.description: Treats the theory of modules and ideals over commutative rings alongside the homological machinery — free resolutions, Tor and Ext, depth, regular sequences — and the chain and finiteness conditions (Noetherian, Artinian) that constrain and organize them. This cluster forms the technical engine of modern commutative algebra, underpinning dimension theory and intersection theory in algebraic geometry.

brief.name: Theory of modules and ideals in commutative rings
brief.definition: Studies modules over commutative rings and the ideals structuring them, including projective, injective, and flat modules, syzygies, and ideal-theoretic invariants.

brief.name: Homological methods in commutative ring theory
brief.definition: Applies homological algebra — free resolutions, Tor, Ext, depth, regular sequences — to commutative rings, yielding invariants such as projective dimension, Cohen-Macaulay, and Gorenstein properties.

brief.name: Chain conditions, finiteness conditions in commutative ring theory
brief.definition: Studies Noetherian, Artinian, and other finiteness or chain conditions on rings and modules that guarantee well-behaved decomposition, primary decomposition, and dimension theory.

blocks.name: Special and Distinguished Classes of Commutative Rings
blocks.description: Classifies commutative rings singled out by arithmetic, factorization, or cardinality constraints: arithmetic rings such as Dedekind and Prüfer domains, integral domains with their factorization theory, and finite commutative rings. Each class carries its own toolkit of invariants, with applications spanning algebraic number theory, valuation theory, and coding theory.

brief.name: Arithmetic rings and other special commutative rings
brief.definition: Studies rings distinguished by arithmetic properties of their ideals, such as Dedekind domains, Prüfer rings, valuation rings, and unique factorization domains.

brief.name: Integral domains
brief.definition: Investigates commutative rings without zero divisors, their divisibility and factorization theory, and classification into UFDs, PIDs, GCD domains, and Krull domains.

brief.name: Finite commutative rings
brief.definition: Studies commutative rings with finitely many elements, their structure and classification, with applications to coding theory and combinatorics.

blocks.name: Topological, Logical, Differential, and Computational Methods
blocks.description: Gathers approaches that import structure or techniques from other fields into commutative algebra: topologies on rings and modules (adic and valuation topologies, completions), model-theoretic and logical methods, differential structures (derivations, differential ideals), and algorithmic techniques such as Gröbner bases. These subclasses mark commutative algebra's expanding interfaces with analysis, mathematical logic, differential equations, and symbolic computation.

brief.name: Topological rings and modules
brief.definition: Studies commutative rings and modules equipped with a compatible topology, such as adic or valuation topologies, and their completions.

brief.name: Applications of logic to commutative algebra
brief.definition: Applies model theory and other branches of mathematical logic to study commutative rings, their elementary theories, and definability of ideals and elements.

brief.name: Differential algebra
brief.definition: Studies commutative rings equipped with derivations, differential ideals, and differential modules, forming the algebraic backbone of differential Galois theory and the theory of differential equations.

brief.name: Computational aspects and applications of commutative rings
brief.definition: Develops algorithms — Gröbner bases, elimination theory, computer algebra systems — for computing with ideals, modules, and rings, with applications throughout algebraic geometry and beyond.

blocks.name: Foundations, Local Theory and Cycles
blocks.description: Covers the scheme-theoretic and sheaf-theoretic foundations on which the rest of algebraic geometry is built, the local structure of varieties and schemes near singular or special points, and the theory of algebraic cycles and subschemes that generalizes divisors to higher codimension. Intersection theory, Chow groups, and deformation theory of singularities sit at the technical core of this block, feeding directly into moduli theory and the study of motives.

brief.name: Foundations of algebraic geometry
brief.definition: Establishes the scheme-theoretic, sheaf-theoretic, and categorical language (schemes, stacks, morphisms) underlying modern algebraic geometry.

brief.name: Local theory in algebraic geometry
brief.definition: Studies the local structure of varieties and schemes, including deformation theory, singularities, and formal/local moduli.

brief.name: Cycles and subschemes
brief.definition: Develops the theory of algebraic cycles, Chow groups, and intersection theory on varieties and schemes.

blocks.name: Families, Fibrations and Cohomology Theories
blocks.description: Concerns varieties that move in families - fibrations, degenerations, and moduli spaces - together with the cohomological invariants (etale, de Rham, crystalline, and motivic) used to detect and classify them. These tools translate geometric variation into linear-algebraic and Galois-theoretic data, underpinning Hodge theory and the theory of motives.

brief.name: Families, fibrations in algebraic geometry
brief.definition: Studies families of varieties, their degenerations, variation of Hodge structure, and moduli of fibered spaces.

brief.name: (Co)homology theory in algebraic geometry
brief.definition: Studies etale, de Rham, crystalline, and motivic (co)homology theories and their sheaf-theoretic foundations for varieties.

blocks.name: Birational Geometry and Classification of Varieties
blocks.description: Addresses the classification of algebraic varieties up to birational equivalence, including the minimal model program, rationality questions, and the finer taxonomy of surfaces, higher-dimensional varieties, and distinguished special classes such as toric, Fano, and Calabi-Yau varieties. This is the geometric core of the Mori-Enriques-Kodaira classification program.

brief.name: Birational geometry
brief.definition: Studies varieties up to birational equivalence via blow-ups, the minimal model program, and rationality problems.

brief.name: Surfaces and higher-dimensional varieties
brief.definition: Classifies algebraic surfaces and higher-dimensional varieties, extending Enriques-Kodaira-type classification schemes.

brief.name: Special varieties
brief.definition: Studies distinguished classes of varieties - toric, determinantal, Grassmannian, complete-intersection, and flag varieties - with rich extra structure.

blocks.name: Curves, Abelian Varieties and Algebraic Groups
blocks.description: Treats the three classical families of varieties carrying group or near-group structure: algebraic curves and their moduli, abelian varieties as projective group varieties, and algebraic groups more generally. This is one of the most mature areas of algebraic geometry, with deep links to number theory, representation theory, and complex analysis via Jacobians and period maps.

brief.name: Curves in algebraic geometry
brief.definition: Studies algebraic curves, their moduli spaces, Jacobians, and the geometry of linear systems on curves.

brief.name: Abelian varieties and schemes
brief.definition: Studies abelian varieties and abelian schemes as projective algebraic group varieties, including polarizations and isogenies.

brief.name: Algebraic groups
brief.definition: Studies algebraic groups and group schemes as geometric objects, complementing their linear and Lie-theoretic treatments.

blocks.name: Arithmetic and Real Algebraic Geometry
blocks.description: Studies algebraic varieties over non-algebraically-closed base fields, split between Diophantine and arithmetic geometry over number fields and finite fields, and real algebraic geometry over the reals with its semialgebraic and o-minimal flavor. Rational-point problems, heights, and Galois representations make this a central bridge to number theory.

brief.name: Arithmetic problems in algebraic geometry; Diophantine geometry
brief.definition: Studies rational and integral points, heights, and Galois representations attached to varieties over arithmetic fields.

brief.name: Real algebraic and real-analytic geometry
brief.definition: Studies varieties and semialgebraic sets defined over the real numbers and their real-analytic counterparts.

blocks.name: Enumerative, Computational, Affine and Tropical Geometry
blocks.description: Groups the more combinatorial and algorithmic corners of algebraic geometry: counting geometric configurations via intersection and Gromov-Witten theory, computing with varieties via effective algebraic algorithms, studying affine varieties and their embeddings, and tropicalizing varieties into piecewise-linear combinatorial shadows. These areas trade heavily with combinatorics, computer algebra, and mirror symmetry.

brief.name: Projective and enumerative algebraic geometry
brief.definition: Studies intersection numbers, Gromov-Witten invariants, and Schubert calculus for counting geometric configurations.

brief.name: Computational aspects in algebraic geometry
brief.definition: Develops algorithms, Grobner-basis methods, and effective computation for varieties and their invariants.

brief.name: Affine geometry
brief.definition: Studies affine varieties, embeddings of affine space, and problems such as the Jacobian conjecture.

brief.name: Tropical geometry
brief.definition: Studies tropicalizations of varieties as piecewise-linear combinatorial shadows encoding their algebraic structure.

pillars.name: Linear and multilinear algebra; matrix theory

blocks.name: Foundations of Linear Algebra
blocks.description: Covers the core structural theory of vector spaces, linear transformations, matrices, determinants, eigenvalues, canonical forms, and multilinear constructions such as tensor and exterior algebras. This is the settled algebraic bedrock underlying nearly every quantitative discipline, from numerical analysis to representation theory. It also includes multilinear algebra topics like tensor products, symmetric and exterior powers, and Clifford algebras insofar as they arise from a matrix/vector-space viewpoint.

brief.name: Basic linear algebra
brief.definition: Studies vector spaces, linear maps, matrices, determinants, eigenvalues/eigenvectors, canonical forms, and multilinear operations such as tensor, symmetric, and exterior products.

blocks.name: Special Matrices and Their Properties
blocks.description: Classifies matrices by structural or spectral properties — symmetric, Hermitian, orthogonal, positive definite, nonnegative, sparse, structured (Toeplitz, Hankel, circulant), and other distinguished families — and studies the inequalities, factorizations, and combinatorial or order-theoretic properties specific to each class. Such classifications underpin numerical stability analysis, combinatorial matrix theory, and applications across statistics, optimization, and control. The area remains actively extended as new structured-matrix classes arise from applied problems.

brief.name: Special matrices
brief.definition: Studies matrices distinguished by structural, spectral, sign, or combinatorial properties, including symmetric, positive definite, nonnegative, orthogonal, and patterned (e.g. Toeplitz, circulant) matrices, together with their associated inequalities and factorizations.

blocks.name: Foundations, Radicals and Structure Theory
blocks.description: Covers the classical backbone of noncommutative ring theory: the general theory of associative rings, the Wedderburn-Artin classification of semisimple Artinian rings and division rings, local and semiperfect rings, and the radical theory (Jacobson, nil, prime) that measures how far a ring departs from semisimplicity. These results form the settled structural vocabulary that every other area of the discipline builds on.

brief.name: General and miscellaneous
brief.definition: Covers foundational notions and cross-cutting results in associative ring theory not assigned to a more specific subclass.

brief.name: Division rings and semisimple Artin rings
brief.definition: Studies rings in which every nonzero element is invertible and Artinian rings with zero Jacobson radical, classified by the Wedderburn-Artin theorem.

brief.name: Local rings and generalizations
brief.definition: Studies associative rings with a unique maximal one-sided ideal and their generalizations, such as semiperfect and semilocal rings.

brief.name: Radicals and radical properties of associative rings
brief.definition: Studies the Jacobson, nil, prime, and other radical ideals that control a ring's departure from semisimplicity and organize its structure theory.

blocks.name: Modules, Homological Methods and Representation Theory
blocks.description: Treats associative algebras through the modules, bimodules, and ideals acting on them, the homological invariants (projective and injective dimension, Ext, Tor, Hochschild cohomology) attached to those modules, and the representation theory that classifies modules via quivers and Auslander-Reiten theory. Tilting theory, derived and cluster categories keep this area under active development well beyond its classical roots.

brief.name: Modules, bimodules and ideals in associative algebras
brief.definition: Studies one-sided and two-sided modules, bimodules, and the lattice of ideals governing associative algebras.

brief.name: Homological methods in associative algebras
brief.definition: Applies homological algebra — projective and injective dimension, Ext, Tor, Hochschild and cyclic cohomology — to associative rings and algebras.

brief.name: Representation theory of associative rings and algebras
brief.definition: Classifies modules over associative algebras using quivers, Auslander-Reiten theory, and related tools for describing indecomposable representations.

blocks.name: Orders, Ring Constructions and Additional Structure
blocks.description: Groups finite-dimensional algebras and their arithmetic orders together with the standard machinery for building new associative rings — group rings, crossed products, Ore and skew-polynomial extensions — and with rings enriched by extra structure such as gradings, filtrations, and involutions. The blend of arithmetic, constructive, and structural techniques keeps parts of this area, notably graded and involutive constructions, in active research.

brief.name: Associative algebras and orders
brief.definition: Studies finite-dimensional associative algebras and orders inside them, linking algebraic structure to arithmetic properties.

brief.name: Associative rings and algebras arising under various constructions
brief.definition: Studies rings produced by standard constructions such as group rings, crossed products, and Ore or skew-polynomial extensions.

brief.name: Associative rings and algebras with additional structure
brief.definition: Studies associative rings equipped with extra structure such as gradings, filtrations, involutions, or group actions.

blocks.name: Polynomial Identities, Chain Conditions and Element Properties
blocks.description: Covers rings governed by finiteness or growth restrictions (Noetherian, Artinian, and related chain conditions), rings satisfying a polynomial identity — a broad generalization of commutativity encompassing all finite-dimensional algebras — and rings characterized by properties of individual elements such as nilpotence, idempotence, or regularity. Kemer's resolution of the Specht problem and the classical chain-condition theory leave this area largely settled, with PI-theoretic refinements ongoing.

brief.name: Chain conditions, growth conditions, and other forms of finiteness
brief.definition: Studies Noetherian, Artinian, and related growth or finiteness conditions imposed on associative rings and algebras.

brief.name: Rings with polynomial identity
brief.definition: Studies rings satisfying a nontrivial polynomial identity, a class generalizing commutative rings and all finite-dimensional algebras.

brief.name: Conditions on elements
brief.definition: Studies rings defined by properties of their elements, such as nilpotents, idempotents, units, and von Neumann regular elements.

blocks.name: Hopf Algebras, Generalizations and Computational Aspects
blocks.description: Covers Hopf algebras and quantum groups — bialgebras with an antipode that generalize group symmetry and underpin quantum topology and noncommutative geometry — together with structures generalizing associative rings, such as near-rings and semirings, and the algorithms and software used to compute with associative rings. Quantum group theory remains a vigorous research frontier tied to representation theory, mathematical physics, and low-dimensional topology.

brief.name: Hopf algebras, quantum groups and related topics
brief.definition: Studies Hopf algebras and quantum groups, bialgebras with a coalgebra structure and antipode that generalize group symmetry.

brief.name: Generalizations
brief.definition: Studies structures generalizing associative rings, such as near-rings and semirings, that relax ring axioms.

brief.name: Computational aspects of associative rings
brief.definition: Studies algorithms, complexity, and software for computing with associative rings and algebras.

blocks.name: General Nonassociative Rings
blocks.description: Studies rings and algebras without the associativity axiom in full generality, developing the structural vocabulary — ideals, radicals, nilpotency, power-associativity, alternativity — that specializes into the Lie, Jordan, and other named subtheories. It provides the unifying framework (identities, varieties of algebras, universal enveloping constructions) against which more specific nonassociative classes are measured. Classical examples include alternative algebras (octonions) and flexible algebras.

brief.name: General nonassociative rings
brief.definition: Studies rings and algebras that need not satisfy the associative law, including their basic structure theory, radicals, and identity-defined varieties such as alternative and flexible algebras.

blocks.name: Lie Algebras and Lie Superalgebras
blocks.description: Covers algebras defined by antisymmetry and the Jacobi identity, together with their Z2-graded (super) generalizations, forming one of the most extensively developed branches of algebra due to deep ties with Lie groups, differential geometry, and mathematical physics. Encompasses classification of simple and semisimple Lie algebras, root systems, representation theory, Kac-Moody and affine Lie algebras, and Lie superalgebras underlying supersymmetry. The finite-dimensional semisimple theory over algebraically closed fields of characteristic zero is essentially complete, while modular, infinite-dimensional, and superalgebra representation theory remain active.

brief.name: Lie algebras and Lie superalgebras
brief.definition: Studies algebras satisfying antisymmetry and the Jacobi identity and their Z2-graded superalgebra analogues, including their classification, structure, and representation theory, with the corresponding Lie groups treated under 22Exx.

blocks.name: Jordan Algebras, Triples, and Pairs
blocks.description: Studies commutative nonassociative algebras satisfying the Jordan identity, originally introduced to axiomatize the algebra of observables in quantum mechanics, along with their generalizations to Jordan triple systems and Jordan pairs used in the theory of symmetric spaces and bounded symmetric domains. Classification of simple Jordan algebras parallels and interlocks with the classification of simple Lie algebras via the Tits-Kantor-Koecher construction. The finite-dimensional theory is classically settled; infinite-dimensional and operator-algebraic Jordan structures see ongoing study.

brief.name: Jordan algebras (algebras, triples and pairs)
brief.definition: Studies commutative algebras satisfying the Jordan identity together with their triple-system and pair generalizations, connecting to symmetric spaces and quantum-mechanical observable algebras.

blocks.name: Other Nonassociative Rings and Algebras
blocks.description: A residual class gathering nonassociative algebraic structures that fall outside the Lie and Jordan paradigms, such as Malcev algebras, quasigroup and loop-related algebras, Leibniz algebras, and other identity-defined nonassociative systems. These structures often arise as deformations or generalizations of Lie/Jordan theory and are studied both for their own structure theory and for applications in geometry and combinatorics. The area is heterogeneous, with pockets of mature theory alongside genuinely open classification questions.

brief.name: Other nonassociative rings and algebras
brief.definition: Covers nonassociative algebraic structures outside the Lie and Jordan families, such as Malcev and Leibniz algebras, unified mainly by not satisfying the standard associativity, antisymmetry-Jacobi, or Jordan identities.

blocks.name: Foundations of Categories and Functors
blocks.description: Establishes the basic language of category theory: objects, morphisms, functors, and natural transformations, together with distinguished classes of categories (toposes, groupoids, fibered categories) and the categorical presentation of algebraic and logical theories via sketches, Lawvere theories, and monads. This block forms the settled, axiomatic bedrock on which all other categorical machinery is built.

brief.name: General theory of categories and functors
brief.definition: Develops the basic language of objects, morphisms, functors, and natural transformations underlying all of category theory.

brief.name: Special categories
brief.definition: Studies distinguished classes of categories, such as toposes, groupoids, ordered categories, and categories of relations, singled out by extra structure or properties.

brief.name: Categories and theories
brief.definition: Studies categorical semantics of algebraic and logical theories through sketches, Lawvere theories, monads, and other presentations of theories as categorical data.

blocks.name: Categorical Structures and Homological Algebra
blocks.description: Covers enriched, internal, and higher-dimensional categorical structures such as bicategories, fibrations, and double categories, alongside the additive and abelian category axiomatics that generalize module and sheaf categories. Builds on these to develop derived categories, triangulated categories, derived functors, and spectral sequences, recasting classical homological algebra in fully categorical terms.

brief.name: Categorical structures
brief.definition: Studies enriched, internal, and higher-dimensional categorical structures, including bicategories, double categories, and fibered categories.

brief.name: Categorical algebra
brief.definition: Studies abelian, additive, and exact categories as the axiomatic framework generalizing categories of modules and sheaves.

brief.name: Homological algebra in category theory, derived categories and functors
brief.definition: Develops derived categories, derived functors, triangulated categories, and spectral sequences as homological algebra formulated categorically.

blocks.name: Monoidal, Higher, and Geometric Categories
blocks.description: Studies categories with a tensor product — monoidal and braided monoidal categories, and the operads governing algebraic operations up to homotopy — together with infinity-categories and other higher-categorical, homotopical structures. Also covers the categorical frameworks, sheaves, stacks, and topos-theoretic geometry, that organize geometry and topology. These areas underpin derived algebraic geometry, topological field theory, and modern homotopy theory.

brief.name: Categories in geometry and topology
brief.definition: Studies sheaves, stacks, and topos-theoretic categorical frameworks used to organize geometric and topological structures.

brief.name: Monoidal categories and operads
brief.definition: Studies categories equipped with a tensor product and the operads that encode algebraic operations and their coherence up to homotopy.

brief.name: Higher categories and homotopical algebra
brief.definition: Studies infinity-categories and other higher-categorical structures together with the homotopical algebra used to model them, a major research frontier.

blocks.name: Classical K-Groups: K₀, K₁, K₂
blocks.description: The foundational low-degree algebraic K-groups of rings and categories: K₀ classifies stable isomorphism classes of projective modules via the Grothendieck construction, K₁ captures automorphisms modulo elementary matrices through Whitehead groups, and K₂ measures relations among elementary matrices via Steinberg groups and symbols. These groups underlie classical applications such as ideal class groups, Whitehead torsion in topology, and Matsumoto's description of K₂ of fields.

brief.name: Grothendieck groups and K_0
brief.definition: Studies K_0(R), the group completion of the monoid of stably free or projective modules over a ring, category, or space, classifying vector bundles and projective modules up to stable isomorphism.

brief.name: Whitehead groups and K_1
brief.definition: Studies K_1(R), generated by automorphisms of free modules modulo elementary-matrix equivalence, encoding units of rings and the Whitehead torsion invariant of homotopy equivalences.

brief.name: Steinberg groups and K_2
brief.definition: Studies K_2(R) via the Steinberg group presentation of relations among elementary matrices, tied to universal central extensions of linear groups and Hilbert symbols in local and global fields.

blocks.name: Higher, Geometric, and Arithmetic K-Theory
blocks.description: Quillen's higher K-groups extend K_0, K_1, K_2 via the plus-construction or Q-construction into a full sequence of functors bridging algebra, geometry, and number theory. This block covers higher algebraic K-theory itself alongside its deployment in geometric settings (bundles, Chow groups, motivic cohomology), arithmetic settings (special values of zeta and L-functions, étale cohomology), and the Hermitian K-theory of quadratic and bilinear forms.

brief.name: Higher algebraic K-theory
brief.definition: Constructs and studies the higher K-groups K_n(R) for n greater than or equal to 2 via Quillen's plus-construction or Q-construction applied to categories of modules or vector bundles.

brief.name: K-theory in geometry
brief.definition: Applies algebraic K-theory to geometric objects such as schemes and vector bundles, connecting K-groups to Chow groups, algebraic cycles, and motivic cohomology.

brief.name: K-theory in number theory
brief.definition: Relates K-groups of rings of integers and number fields to arithmetic invariants including class numbers, special zeta and L-function values, and étale cohomology, as in the Quillen-Lichtenbaum and Bloch-Kato conjectures.

brief.name: K-theory of forms
brief.definition: Studies Hermitian and quadratic K-theory, the K-theoretic classification of symmetric, skew-symmetric, and quadratic bilinear forms over rings equipped with an involution.

blocks.name: Topological K-Theory and Its Obstructions
blocks.description: Topological K-theory assigns to a space the Grothendieck group of complex or real vector bundles over it, forming an extraordinary cohomology theory governed by Bott periodicity and central to index theory and stable homotopy computations. Closely related obstruction-theoretic invariants in low-degree K-groups detect failures of finiteness or simple-homotopy equivalence in manifold and CW-complex topology.

brief.name: Obstructions from topology
brief.definition: Studies K-theoretic obstructions to geometric problems, notably the Wall finiteness obstruction and Whitehead torsion, which control finiteness and simple-homotopy equivalence of spaces.

brief.name: Topological K-theory
brief.definition: Studies the K^0 and K^1 functors of complex or real vector bundles over topological spaces, Bott periodicity, and the resulting generalized cohomology theory used throughout index theory.

blocks.name: K-Theory of Operator Algebras and Applications
blocks.description: K-theory of C*-algebras and von Neumann algebras, including bivariant KK-theory, classifies operator algebras and drives the Baum-Connes and Novikov conjectures linking functional analysis, geometry, and topology. This block also gathers miscellaneous further applications of K-theoretic methods that fall outside the primary algebraic, geometric, arithmetic, and topological classifications.

brief.name: K-theory and operator algebras
brief.definition: Studies K-groups and Kasparov's bivariant KK-theory of C*-algebras and von Neumann algebras, foundational to noncommutative geometry and operator-algebraic index theory.

brief.name: Miscellaneous applications of K-theory
brief.definition: Covers applications of K-theoretic methods to problems that do not fall under the primary algebraic, geometric, arithmetic, or operator-algebraic classifications above.

blocks.name: Foundations and Structural Classification
blocks.description: Establishes the axiomatic language of group theory and develops the structure and classification theory of both finite and infinite groups. Includes the machinery of subgroup lattices, composition series, and extension theory that underlies the largely completed classification of finite simple groups, alongside special structural properties such as nilpotency, solvability, growth, and geometric group theory that remain actively studied for infinite groups. Serves as the conceptual backbone from which every other block in 20-XX draws its basic vocabulary.

brief.name: Foundations
brief.definition: Covers the basic axioms, generators-and-relations presentations, and elementary constructions (subgroups, quotients, homomorphisms) on which all group theory is built.

brief.name: Abstract finite groups
brief.definition: Studies the internal structure of finite groups, including Sylow theory, solvable and nilpotent groups, and the composition-factor structure feeding into simple-group classification.

brief.name: Structure and classification of infinite or finite groups
brief.definition: Develops general subgroup-structure and classification results applicable to both finite and infinite groups, including chief series, maximal subgroups, and extension problems.

brief.name: Special aspects of infinite or finite groups
brief.definition: Investigates specialized group-theoretic properties such as word and conjugacy problems, subgroup growth, and geometric/combinatorial group theory for finitely generated and infinite groups.

blocks.name: Group Actions, Representations, and Linear Algebraic Groups
blocks.description: Concerns groups realized as symmetries of other structures: permutation actions on sets, linear representations on vector spaces, and algebraic groups defined by polynomial equations. Representation theory and the theory of linear algebraic groups form deep, mutually reinforcing pillars connecting group theory to combinatorics, geometry, and number theory, with reductive-group theory and character theory remaining major active research programs. Matrix groups arising outside the algebraic-group framework, such as classical and arithmetic groups, round out the concrete, computable end of the spectrum.

brief.name: Permutation groups
brief.definition: Studies groups acting on sets, focusing on transitivity, primitivity, orbits, and the combinatorial structure of permutation representations.

brief.name: Representation theory of groups
brief.definition: Analyzes homomorphisms of groups into groups of linear transformations, encompassing ordinary and modular character theory and modules over group rings and algebras.

brief.name: Linear algebraic groups and related topics
brief.definition: Studies algebraic groups defined by polynomial equations over a field, including reductive and semisimple groups, their representation theory, and arithmetic and geometric variants.

brief.name: Other groups of matrices
brief.definition: Covers concrete matrix groups such as classical groups, arithmetic groups, and discrete matrix subgroups studied outside the full algebraic-group apparatus.

blocks.name: Abelian Groups, Homological Connections, and Groupoids
blocks.description: Gathers the commutative branch of group theory together with its categorical and cohomological extensions. Abelian group theory is a classical, essentially settled subject built on invariants like rank and torsion, while the study of group cohomology, extensions, and categorical structures such as groupoids remains an active bridge to homological algebra and higher category theory. Groupoids generalize groups to multi-object categories with invertible morphisms, unifying symmetry with more flexible partial structure.

brief.name: Abelian groups
brief.definition: Studies commutative groups, classifying them via invariants such as rank, torsion, and divisibility, for both finitely and infinitely generated cases.

brief.name: Connections of group theory with homological algebra and category theory
brief.definition: Examines group cohomology and homology, crossed modules, and categorical constructions such as extensions that link group theory to homological algebra.

brief.name: Groupoids
brief.definition: Studies groupoids, small categories in which every morphism is invertible, generalizing groups to structures with multiple objects and partial composition.

blocks.name: Semigroups, Generalized Structures, and Probabilistic Methods
blocks.description: Extends the group concept in two directions: algebraically, by relaxing invertibility to obtain semigroups and other group-like structures such as quasigroups and loops; and methodologically, by importing probability theory to study random walks and asymptotic behavior on groups. Semigroup theory is a mature classical subject, while probabilistic group theory is a comparatively young and fast-moving research frontier with deep ties to ergodic theory and geometric group theory. Together these topics push group theory's reach beyond its strict axiomatic core.

brief.name: Semigroups
brief.definition: Studies sets equipped with an associative binary operation without a general inverse requirement, including monoids and inverse semigroups and their structure theory.

brief.name: Other generalizations of groups
brief.definition: Covers algebraic structures generalizing groups beyond semigroups and groupoids, such as quasigroups, loops, and other non-associative or partial generalizations.

brief.name: Probabilistic methods in group theory
brief.definition: Applies probability theory to group theory, studying random walks on groups, random subgroups, and asymptotic statistical properties of group elements and structures.

blocks.name: Topological and Locally Compact Group Theory
blocks.description: Studies groups (and more general algebraic systems) equipped with a compatible topology, focusing on the structural interplay between algebra and topology. Core results include the Pontryagin duality theorem for locally compact abelian groups, the Peter-Weyl theorem for compact groups, and the general structure theory of locally compact groups via their group algebras and unitary representations. This area furnishes the foundational machinery for abstract harmonic analysis and underlies much of modern representation theory.

brief.name: Topological and differentiable algebraic systems
brief.definition: Studies semigroups, quasigroups, and other algebraic systems endowed with a compatible topological or differentiable structure, excluding topological rings and fields.

brief.name: Locally compact abelian groups (LCA groups)
brief.definition: Develops the structure theory of abelian topological groups that are locally compact, centered on the Pontryagin duality between an LCA group and its character group.

brief.name: Compact groups
brief.definition: Studies compact topological groups via the Peter-Weyl theorem, decomposing their representations into finite-dimensional irreducibles and describing their structure as inverse limits of Lie groups.

brief.name: Locally compact groups and their algebras
brief.definition: Develops the general structure and unitary representation theory of locally compact groups, including their group algebras, induced representations, and amenability properties.

blocks.name: Lie Groups and Noncompact Transformation Groups
blocks.description: Concerns Lie groups — groups that are simultaneously smooth manifolds with smooth multiplication — their associated Lie algebras, structure theory, and representations, together with the actions of noncompact groups on spaces. This area bridges algebra, differential geometry, and analysis, and its representation theory of noncompact semisimple Lie groups (unitary duals, discrete series, geometric actions) remains an active research frontier with several classification problems still open.

brief.name: Lie groups
brief.definition: Studies Lie groups as smooth manifolds with compatible group structure, together with their Lie algebras, structure theory, and finite- and infinite-dimensional representations.

brief.name: Noncompact transformation groups
brief.definition: Examines actions of noncompact topological or Lie groups on spaces, including orbit structure, proper actions, and the geometric and dynamical consequences of noncompactness.

blocks.name: Single- and Multivariable Real Function Theory
blocks.description: Studies the local and global behavior of real-valued functions of one or several real variables: continuity, differentiability, monotonicity, convexity, absolute continuity, bounded variation, and generalized derivatives. This is the classical descriptive backbone of real analysis, underlying calculus, measure theory, and the analytic treatment of functions on R and R^n.

brief.name: Functions of one variable
brief.definition: The classical theory of real-valued functions of a single real variable, covering continuity, differentiability, monotonicity, convexity, absolute continuity, bounded variation, and generalized notions of derivative.

brief.name: Functions of several variables
brief.definition: The multivariable extension of one-variable real analysis, studying partial and directional derivatives, differentiability, Jacobians, convexity, and change-of-variables results for functions on R^n.

blocks.name: Polynomials and Rational Functions in Real Analysis
blocks.description: Examines real polynomials and rational functions through an analytic lens: root location and separation, extremal and shape properties, factorization, and behavior under approximation on real intervals. It bridges classical algebra with real-variable analytic techniques such as interpolation and Chebyshev-type extremal problems.

brief.name: Polynomials, rational functions in real analysis
brief.definition: The analytic study of real polynomials and rational functions, including root location, extremal and sign properties, and their approximation-theoretic behavior on the real line.

blocks.name: Inequalities in Real Analysis
blocks.description: Develops and classifies analytic inequalities for real functions — convexity-based, mean-value, integral, and comparison inequalities — that quantify relationships between a function's values, derivatives, or integrals. Excludes maximal-function inequalities (42B25), functional inequalities (39B72), and probabilistic inequalities (60E15), which belong to their own disciplines despite overlapping techniques.

brief.name: Inequalities in real analysis
brief.definition: The systematic study of classical and modern analytic inequalities for real functions, such as convexity, mean-value, and integral inequalities, distinct from maximal-function, functional, and probabilistic inequalities treated in other classes.

blocks.name: Miscellaneous and Non-Classical Real Function Topics
blocks.description: A residual category for real-function topics outside the classical descriptive and inequality theory, including nonstandard-analysis treatments, o-minimal and tame-geometry perspectives, and other emerging analytic frameworks. It serves as the growth point of the discipline, absorbing new approaches before they mature into dedicated subclasses.

brief.name: Miscellaneous topics in real functions
brief.definition: A catch-all subclass for non-classical or emerging approaches to real functions — such as nonstandard analysis and o-minimal structures — not covered by the other 26-XX subclasses.

blocks.name: Foundations of Measure and Integration
blocks.description: Covers the construction of measures and integrals starting from Lebesgue's framework on Euclidean space, their extension to abstract vector-space-valued set functions, and their specialization to spaces carrying topological, group, or infinite-dimensional structure. Also gathers auxiliary and non-standard measure-theoretic constructions that fall outside the main lines. Together these subclasses supply the analytic bedrock — sigma-algebras, integration, Radon-Nikodym-type differentiation, Haar and Gaussian measures — on which probability theory, functional analysis, and harmonic analysis are built.

brief.name: Classical measure theory
brief.definition: Develops the Lebesgue framework of sigma-algebras, measurable functions, and integration on Euclidean and abstract sets, including convergence theorems, product measures, and the Radon-Nikodym derivative.

brief.name: Set functions, measures and integrals with values in abstract spaces
brief.definition: Extends measure and integration theory to set functions and integrands valued in Banach spaces or other abstract targets, via vector measures and the Bochner and Pettis integrals.

brief.name: Set functions and measures on spaces with additional structure
brief.definition: Studies measures compatible with extra ambient structure, such as Radon measures on topological spaces, Haar measure on locally compact groups, and Gaussian or cylindrical measures on infinite-dimensional spaces.

brief.name: Miscellaneous topics in measure theory
brief.definition: Collects measure-theoretic constructions outside the standard hierarchy, including finitely additive set functions and non-standard approaches to measure and integration.

blocks.name: Measure-Theoretic Ergodic Theory
blocks.description: Studies measure-preserving transformations and flows on probability spaces, treating invariant measures, entropy, mixing, and recurrence as the central objects of analysis. It forms the measure-theoretic core of dynamical systems theory, feeding into number theory, statistical mechanics, and probability via the ergodic theorem and its generalizations. The area remains a vigorous research frontier, with deep open problems on rigidity, entropy classification, and connections to number-theoretic dynamics.

brief.name: Measure-theoretic ergodic theory
brief.definition: Analyzes measure-preserving transformations and flows through invariant measures, entropy, mixing properties, and recurrence, forming the measure-theoretic core of dynamical systems.

blocks.name: Foundations, Series, and General Theory
blocks.description: Covers the core toolkit of one-variable complex analysis: holomorphy, analytic continuation, boundary behavior, and the Taylor, Laurent, and Dirichlet series representations that encode a holomorphic function's local and global structure. Also gathers miscellaneous extremal and structural questions in the complex plane that do not fit a more specialized subclass. This is the settled bedrock on which every other 30-XX area builds.

brief.name: General properties of functions of one complex variable
brief.definition: Studies the basic structural properties of holomorphic and meromorphic functions of a single complex variable, including continuity, differentiability, and analytic continuation.

brief.name: Series expansions of functions of one complex variable
brief.definition: Studies power, Laurent, Dirichlet, and other series representations of holomorphic functions, together with their convergence and summability properties.

brief.name: Miscellaneous topics of analysis in the complex plane
brief.definition: Collects extremal problems, approximation questions, and other complex-plane analysis topics not classified under a dedicated 30-XX subclass.

blocks.name: Geometric Function Theory and Value Distribution
blocks.description: Examines how holomorphic maps distort shape and measure — conformal and quasiconformal mappings, univalence, extremal length, and coefficient problems descending from the Bieberbach conjecture — alongside the global growth and value-distribution theory of entire and meromorphic functions built on Nevanlinna theory. Together these areas connect complex analysis to geometry, dynamics, and Diophantine approximation. Nevanlinna theory and its ramifications remain an active research frontier.

brief.name: Geometric function theory
brief.definition: Studies conformal and quasiconformal mappings, univalent functions, and the geometric distortion properties of holomorphic maps between planar domains.

brief.name: Entire and meromorphic functions of one complex variable, and related topics
brief.definition: Studies the growth, factorization, and value distribution of entire and meromorphic functions via Nevanlinna theory and related methods.

blocks.name: Riemann Surfaces, the Disc, and Analytic Function Spaces
blocks.description: Covers Riemann surfaces as the natural domains of multi-valued and uniformized holomorphic functions, together with the Banach and Hilbert spaces and algebras of holomorphic functions — Hardy, Bergman, Dirichlet, and disc-algebra theory — built on the unit disc as the model domain. These areas connect complex analysis to functional analysis, operator theory, and uniformization. The disc-space theory is classical while moduli-theoretic aspects of Riemann surfaces remain actively studied.

brief.name: Riemann surfaces
brief.definition: Studies one-dimensional complex manifolds, their uniformization, coverings, and moduli, as the natural setting for multi-valued holomorphic functions.

brief.name: Spaces and algebras of analytic functions of one complex variable
brief.definition: Studies Banach and Hilbert spaces and algebras of holomorphic functions, such as Hardy, Bergman, and Dirichlet spaces, and their operator-theoretic properties.

brief.name: Function theory on the disc
brief.definition: Studies holomorphic functions on the unit disc, including boundary value behavior, inner-outer factorization, and Blaschke products.

blocks.name: Generalized, Universal, and Metric-Space Complex Analysis
blocks.description: Extends classical complex analysis beyond the holomorphic setting: generalized function theory embeds hyperfunctions and distributional boundary values into complex analytic frameworks, universal holomorphic function theory studies functions whose translates or dilates approximate arbitrary targets, and analysis on metric spaces transplants conformal and quasiconformal ideas to general metric measure spaces lacking a linear structure. These are comparatively young, actively researched extensions with open structural questions.

brief.name: Generalized function theory
brief.definition: Studies extensions of complex function theory to hyperfunctions, distributions, and other generalized-function frameworks.

brief.name: Universal holomorphic functions of one complex variable
brief.definition: Studies holomorphic functions whose partial sums, translates, or dilates can approximate arbitrary functions, i.e. universality and hypercyclicity phenomena.

brief.name: Analysis on metric spaces
brief.definition: Studies quasiconformal and conformally invariant analysis on general metric measure spaces, generalizing planar complex-analytic structures beyond the classical linear setting.

blocks.name: Classical Euclidean Potential Theory
blocks.description: Studies harmonic and subharmonic functions, Green's functions, capacity, and boundary-value problems generated by the Laplacian in the plane and in higher-dimensional Euclidean space. This is the classical core of potential theory, born from Newtonian gravitation and 19th-century complex analysis, and it underlies the Dirichlet problem, harmonic measure, and the theory of superharmonic functions used throughout PDE and complex analysis. Two-dimensional theory draws heavily on conformal mapping and complex-analytic techniques absent in higher dimensions, where the subject connects instead to elliptic PDE and geometric measure theory.

brief.name: Two-dimensional potential theory
brief.definition: Studies harmonic, subharmonic, and superharmonic functions of two real variables and their associated Green's and Poisson kernels, exploiting the special link to complex analysis and conformal mapping.

brief.name: Higher-dimensional potential theory
brief.definition: Extends classical potential theory to functions of three or more variables, treating Newtonian potentials, capacity, harmonic measure, and the Dirichlet problem for the Laplacian in Euclidean space.

blocks.name: Generalized and Axiomatic Potential Theory
blocks.description: Develops potential theory beyond the classical Laplacian by replacing harmonic functions with solutions of more general elliptic or parabolic operators, and by axiomatizing the essential structural properties (sheaf axioms, convergence axioms, balayage) that a theory of harmonic sheaves must satisfy. The axiomatic approach, pioneered by Brelot, Bauer, and Constantinescu-Cornea, unifies classical, Riesz, and more exotic potential theories under a common sheaf-theoretic framework and clarifies exactly which analytic facts are structural versus incidental. It provides the abstract scaffolding later exploited by potential theory on general state spaces, including Dirichlet-form and Markov-process approaches.

brief.name: Generalizations of potential theory
brief.definition: Covers potential-theoretic frameworks attached to operators other than the classical Laplacian, such as polyharmonic, Riesz, and pluripotential theories, and their associated capacities and kernels.

brief.name: Axiomatic potential theory
brief.definition: Formulates potential theory abstractly via axioms on sheaves of harmonic functions (e.g. the Brelot and Bauer axiom systems), isolating the minimal structural hypotheses needed for a Green-function and boundary theory.

blocks.name: Potential Theory on Fractals and Metric Spaces
blocks.description: Constructs Dirichlet forms, Laplacians, and capacities on spaces lacking classical smooth structure, such as self-similar fractals, graphs, and general metric measure spaces satisfying doubling and Poincaré-type conditions. It generalizes harmonic analysis to settings like the Sierpiński gasket where differential-geometric tools fail, replacing them with resistance metrics, heat-kernel estimates, and analysis on metric measure spaces. The field remains a very active research frontier connecting probability, geometric analysis, and mathematical physics, with many analogues of classical results still being established.

brief.name: Potential theory on fractals and metric spaces
brief.definition: Studies Laplacians, Dirichlet forms, capacities, and harmonic functions on fractal sets and general metric measure spaces, replacing smooth differential structure with resistance forms and heat-kernel methods.

pillars.name: Several complex variables and analytic spaces

blocks.name: Function Theory and Analytic Continuation
blocks.description: Covers the core function theory of several complex variables: power-series and integral representations, growth and boundary behavior of holomorphic functions, and the phenomena — such as Hartogs extension — governing when a holomorphic function forced to extend beyond its original domain. Also includes non-Archimedean analytic function theory, the valued-field analogue of classical holomorphy, which is typically studied jointly with a companion classification tag. This block forms the classical foundation on which the rest of the discipline's convexity and geometry theories are built.

brief.name: Holomorphic functions of several complex variables
brief.definition: Studies power series, integral representations, growth, and boundary behavior of holomorphic functions of several complex variables.

brief.name: Analytic continuation
brief.definition: Studies extension of holomorphic functions and analytic objects beyond their initial domain of definition, including Hartogs-type forced-extension phenomena.

brief.name: Non-Archimedean analysis
brief.definition: Studies analytic function theory over non-Archimedean valued fields, the several-variable analogue of rigid and Berkovich analytic geometry.

blocks.name: Local Analytic Geometry, Analytic Spaces and Singularities
blocks.description: Studies the local and global structure of analytic sets and spaces: germs of analytic varieties near a point, the ringed-space formalism of general analytic spaces, generalized (e.g. Banach or formal) variants of that formalism, and the singular points where analytic varieties fail to be manifolds. Singularity theory in particular remains a vigorous research area, linking local algebra, topology of links, and resolution techniques to algebraic geometry. Together these subclasses supply the geometric-algebraic scaffolding — local rings, structure sheaves, singularity invariants — used throughout complex analytic geometry.

brief.name: Local analytic geometry
brief.definition: Studies the local structure of analytic sets and germs of analytic spaces via Weierstrass preparation and local rings of holomorphic functions.

brief.name: Analytic spaces
brief.definition: Develops the general theory of analytic spaces as ringed spaces locally modeled on analytic sets, admitting nilpotent structure sheaves.

brief.name: Generalizations of analytic spaces
brief.definition: Studies generalized notions of analytic space, such as Banach-analytic or formal variants, extending the classical local-ringed-space formalism.

brief.name: Complex singularities
brief.definition: Studies singular points of complex analytic varieties, their resolution, the topology of their links, and invariants such as Milnor fibers.

blocks.name: Convexity, Pseudoconvex Domains and Pluripotential Theory
