blocks.description: Studies the convexity notions unique to several complex variables: holomorphic convexity and the Levi problem, geometric and metric formulations of convexity, and the pseudoconvex domains that are the natural setting for holomorphic extension. Pluripotential theory extends classical potential theory via plurisubharmonic functions and the complex Monge-Ampère operator, underpinning modern work on Kähler-Einstein metrics and singular geometry. The interplay between domain geometry and potential-theoretic tools keeps this cluster an active engine of the field.

brief.name: Holomorphic convexity
brief.definition: Studies domains and spaces that are holomorphically convex, including domains of holomorphy and the Levi problem.

brief.name: Geometric convexity in several complex variables
brief.definition: Studies geometric and metric formulations of convexity, including curvature characterizations, specific to several complex variables.

brief.name: Pseudoconvex domains
brief.definition: Studies domains characterized by pseudoconvexity, the analytic condition governing holomorphic extendability and boundary regularity.

brief.name: Pluripotential theory
brief.definition: Studies plurisubharmonic functions, complex Monge-Ampère operators, and pluripolar sets as the several-variable analogue of potential theory.

blocks.name: CR Manifolds and Differential Operators
blocks.description: Studies Cauchy-Riemann manifolds — the real hypersurfaces that arise as boundaries of complex domains — together with the intrinsic CR structures they carry, and the differential operators, chiefly the ∂-bar operator and the ∂-bar-Neumann problem, used to solve extension and regularity questions on them. CR embeddability and sharp subelliptic estimates for these operators remain genuinely open in many settings. This block bridges the boundary geometry of pseudoconvex domains with the analytic machinery needed to work on it.

brief.name: CR manifolds
brief.definition: Studies Cauchy-Riemann manifolds, the real hypersurfaces bounding complex domains together with their intrinsic CR structure.

brief.name: Differential operators in several variables
brief.definition: Studies the ∂-bar operator, the ∂-bar-Neumann problem, and other differential operators intrinsic to several-complex-variable analysis.

blocks.name: Global Geometry: Compact Spaces, Complex Manifolds and Deformations
blocks.description: Studies complex objects in the large: compact analytic spaces and their classification, the differential-geometric and topological properties of complex manifolds including Kähler geometry, and the deformation theory that governs families and moduli of complex structures via Kodaira-Spencer theory and period maps. Moduli and deformation questions remain a highly active research front connecting complex geometry to algebraic geometry and mathematical physics. This block captures the discipline's global, moduli-theoretic dimension.

brief.name: Deformations of analytic structures
brief.definition: Studies families and moduli of complex analytic structures via Kodaira-Spencer deformation theory and period maps.

brief.name: Compact analytic spaces
brief.definition: Studies compact complex analytic spaces and manifolds, their classification, and cohomological invariants.

brief.name: Complex manifolds
brief.definition: Studies the differential-geometric and topological properties of complex manifolds, including Kähler geometry and curvature.

blocks.name: Holomorphic Mappings, Fiber Spaces and Automorphism Groups
blocks.description: Studies holomorphic maps and correspondences between complex spaces and their iteration and value-distribution behavior, holomorphic fiber bundles and the sheaf cohomology used to classify them, complex spaces distinguished by a large automorphism group such as homogeneous and symmetric spaces, and automorphic functions invariant under discrete group actions that generalize classical automorphic forms. Fiber-bundle and mapping theory here rest on well-established classical foundations. Together these subclasses describe how complex spaces relate to one another and to the symmetries acting on them.

brief.name: Holomorphic mappings and correspondences
brief.definition: Studies holomorphic maps and multivalued correspondences between complex spaces, including their fixed points, iteration, and value distribution.

brief.name: Holomorphic fiber spaces
brief.definition: Studies holomorphic fiber bundles and fiber spaces, including line and vector bundles and sheaf cohomology over complex spaces.

brief.name: Complex spaces with a group of automorphisms
brief.definition: Studies complex manifolds and spaces equipped with a large or transitive automorphism group, including homogeneous and symmetric spaces.

brief.name: Automorphic functions
brief.definition: Studies functions invariant under discrete groups acting on complex domains, generalizing classical automorphic forms to several variables.

blocks.name: Elementary and Classical Transcendental Functions
blocks.description: Studies the trigonometric, exponential, logarithmic, gamma, beta, and elliptic-type elementary transcendental functions that form the bedrock vocabulary of analysis. These functions arise as solutions of the simplest differential and functional equations and underlie virtually every other class of special function. Their identities, asymptotics, and functional equations were largely settled in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries but remain indispensable computational and theoretical tools.

brief.name: Elementary classical functions
brief.definition: The class of trigonometric, exponential, logarithmic, gamma, beta, and related elementary transcendental functions together with their identities and functional equations.

blocks.name: Hypergeometric and Basic (q-) Hypergeometric Functions
blocks.description: Covers the Gauss hypergeometric function, its generalizations (Appell, Lauricella, and generalized pFq series), and their q-deformed basic hypergeometric analogues, which encode solutions to broad families of linear differential and difference equations. These series unify orthogonal polynomials, combinatorial identities, and integral transforms under a single algebraic framework, with q-series linking to quantum groups and modular forms. The classical theory is mature, but q-hypergeometric identities and their combinatorial and representation-theoretic ramifications remain an active research area.

brief.name: Hypergeometric functions
brief.definition: Functions defined by hypergeometric series and their generalizations (Gauss, Appell, Lauricella, generalized pFq), satisfying linear differential equations with regular singular points.

brief.name: Basic hypergeometric functions
brief.definition: The q-analogue theory of hypergeometric functions, built from basic (q-)series and satisfying q-difference equations, connecting to quantum algebra and modular forms.

blocks.name: Other Special Functions and Their Computation
blocks.description: Encompasses special functions outside the elementary and hypergeometric hierarchies, such as Bessel-type, Airy, Painleve transcendents, and other functions defined by specific differential, integral, or functional equations, together with the numerical and symbolic methods used to evaluate them reliably. Computational aspects address algorithms, error bounds, and software strategies for stable evaluation across parameter regimes. The catalogue of named functions is well established, but algorithmic efficiency and rigorous error analysis for extreme parameter ranges remain areas of ongoing refinement.

brief.name: Other special functions
brief.definition: Special functions not classified as elementary or hypergeometric, including Bessel-type functions, Painleve transcendents, and other functions arising from specific differential or integral equations.

brief.name: Computational aspects of special functions
brief.definition: The design and analysis of numerical and symbolic algorithms for evaluating special functions accurately and efficiently across their parameter domains.

blocks.name: Foundational, Boundary-Value, and Qualitative Theory
blocks.description: The classical core of ODE analysis: existence, uniqueness, and continuation of solutions; behavior under two-point or multi-point boundary constraints; the geometric long-term structure of trajectories (phase portraits, limit cycles, bifurcations); sensitivity to perturbation via stability theory; and asymptotic approximation of solutions near singularities or extreme parameter regimes. This is settled, textbook-level mathematics forming the substrate on which every other ODE subfield builds.

brief.name: General theory for ordinary differential equations
brief.definition: Establishes existence, uniqueness, continuation, and elementary solution techniques for scalar and vector ODEs, including Picard–Lindelöf and Peano-type theorems.

brief.name: Boundary value problems for ordinary differential equations
brief.definition: Studies existence, uniqueness, and multiplicity of solutions to ODEs subject to conditions imposed at two or more points, including Sturm–Liouville and nonlinear eigenvalue problems.

brief.name: Qualitative theory for ordinary differential equations
brief.definition: Investigates the geometric and long-term behavior of solutions—phase portraits, periodic orbits, limit cycles, and bifurcations—without requiring explicit integration.

brief.name: Stability theory for ordinary differential equations
brief.definition: Analyzes how solutions respond to perturbations of initial data or parameters using Lyapunov functions, linearization, and related stability criteria.

brief.name: Asymptotic theory for ordinary differential equations
brief.definition: Develops perturbation methods, WKB approximations, and singular-perturbation techniques to describe solution behavior near singularities or in limiting parameter regimes.

blocks.name: Generalized, Stochastic, and Applied ODE Frameworks
blocks.description: Extensions of the ordinary differential equation framework to richer settings that interface with neighboring fields: equations perturbed by randomness, equations valued in infinite-dimensional abstract spaces, equations with control inputs for engineering applications, equations with memory or delay, and dynamics unified across continuous and discrete time via time scales. Each subfield imports methods from a bordering discipline—probability, functional analysis, control theory, or dynamical systems—and remains an active site of development.

brief.name: Ordinary differential equations and systems with randomness
brief.definition: Extends deterministic ODE theory to equations whose coefficients, inputs, or driving terms are random, bridging to stochastic analysis.

brief.name: Differential equations in abstract spaces
brief.definition: Generalizes ODEs to equations for functions valued in Banach or Hilbert spaces, unifying finite-dimensional ODE theory with functional-analytic and PDE methods.

brief.name: Control problems including ordinary differential equations
brief.definition: Studies ODE systems driven by control inputs, addressing controllability, optimal control, and feedback stabilization.

brief.name: Functional-differential equations
brief.definition: Covers equations whose evolution depends on past, future, or state-dependent history values, including delay, advanced, and neutral differential equations.

brief.name: Dynamic equations on time scales or measure chains
brief.definition: Unifies continuous ODE theory and discrete difference-equation theory by formulating dynamics on arbitrary closed subsets of the real line.

blocks.name: Differential Operators and the Complex Domain
blocks.description: The operator-theoretic and complex-analytic wings of ODE theory. 34Lxx treats linear differential expressions as operators on function spaces, developing spectral theory and eigenvalue distribution results in the tradition of Sturm–Liouville theory. 34Mxx studies equations whose independent variable ranges over the complex plane, where singularity structure, monodromy, and the Painlevé property govern solution behavior and give rise to special transcendental functions.

brief.name: Ordinary differential operators
brief.definition: Studies linear differential expressions as operators on function spaces, including self-adjointness, spectral theory, and eigenvalue asymptotics.

brief.name: Ordinary differential equations in the complex domain
brief.definition: Studies ODEs with complex independent variable, focusing on singularity analysis, monodromy, the Painlevé property, and associated special functions.

blocks.name: Foundations and General Theory
blocks.description: Covers the field's cross-cutting methodological apparatus: general existence/uniqueness frameworks, the qualitative behavior of solutions (regularity, asymptotics, comparison principles), explicit and integral representation formulas, and the various notions of generalized (weak, distributional, viscosity) solutions that make modern PDE theory rigorous. These four subclasses supply the vocabulary and toolkit that every other block in 35-XX draws on rather than studying a single equation type.

brief.name: General topics in partial differential equations
brief.definition: Covers foundational and cross-cutting questions in PDE theory not tied to a specific equation type, such as general existence, uniqueness, and well-posedness frameworks.

brief.name: Qualitative properties of solutions to partial differential equations
brief.definition: Studies structural behavior of PDE solutions — regularity, stability, asymptotics, maximum principles, blow-up, and comparison results — independent of explicit formulas.

brief.name: Representations of solutions to partial differential equations
brief.definition: Develops explicit formulas, integral representations, series expansions, and transform methods that express solutions of PDEs in closed or semi-closed form.

brief.name: Generalized solutions to partial differential equations
brief.definition: Formalizes weak, distributional, viscosity, and other generalized solution concepts needed when classical differentiable solutions fail to exist.

blocks.name: Structural Classification by Order and Coefficients
blocks.description: Organizes PDEs and systems by their algebraic and differential-order structure rather than by analytic type: equations with constant coefficients (amenable to Fourier and algebraic methods), general first-order equations and systems (the domain of characteristics and Hamilton–Jacobi theory), and general higher-order equations lacking a clean elliptic/parabolic/hyperbolic classification. This block sits between the general foundational tools and the classical type-based theories, handling cases defined by structural rather than qualitative criteria.

brief.name: Partial differential equations and systems of partial differential equations with constant coefficients
brief.definition: Studies PDEs and systems whose coefficients are constant, a setting where Fourier and algebraic techniques yield sharp existence and structure results.

brief.name: General first-order partial differential equations and systems of first-order partial differential equations
brief.definition: Treats general first-order PDEs and systems, including quasilinear and fully nonlinear cases solved via the method of characteristics and Hamilton–Jacobi theory.

brief.name: General higher-order partial differential equations and systems of higher-order partial differential equations
brief.definition: Covers general PDEs and systems of order three or higher that do not fall neatly into the elliptic, parabolic, or hyperbolic classification.

blocks.name: Classical Equation Types by Principal Part
blocks.description: Encompasses the canonical trichotomy of PDE theory — elliptic, parabolic, and hyperbolic equations and systems — together with the borderline close-to-elliptic and mixed-type classes that interpolate between them. Classification by the principal symbol determines the qualitative behavior (smoothing, propagation, equilibrium) and the appropriate function-space and boundary/initial-value framework for each equation, making this the analytic backbone of the discipline.

brief.name: Close-to-elliptic equations
brief.definition: Studies equations and systems that fail to be strictly elliptic but retain elliptic-like structure, such as subelliptic and Hörmander-type operators.

brief.name: Elliptic equations and elliptic systems
brief.definition: Studies equations and systems characterized by a positive-definite principal symbol, governing equilibrium phenomena and boundary-value problems.

brief.name: Parabolic equations and parabolic systems
brief.definition: Studies equations and systems combining an elliptic spatial part with first-order time evolution, modeling diffusion and smoothing processes.

brief.name: Hyperbolic equations and hyperbolic systems
brief.definition: Studies equations and systems governing wave propagation with finite speed, characterized by real, generally simple characteristics.

brief.name: Partial differential equations of mixed type and mixed-type systems of partial differential equations
brief.definition: Studies equations and systems whose type (elliptic, parabolic, or hyperbolic) changes across the domain, such as Tricomi-type equations.

blocks.name: Spectral and Overdetermined Problems
blocks.description: Addresses two special problem structures layered on top of a given PDE: overdetermined boundary-value problems, where more conditions are imposed than a generic solution can satisfy — forcing rigidity results about the domain or coefficients — and spectral/eigenvalue theory, which studies the discrete or continuous spectrum of differential operators and underlies separation-of-variables and stability analysis. Both connect PDE theory tightly to operator theory and geometric rigidity.

brief.name: Overdetermined problems for partial differential equations and systems of partial differential equations
brief.definition: Studies PDE boundary-value problems with more prescribed conditions than a generic solution admits, typically yielding rigidity or symmetry results on the domain.

brief.name: Spectral theory and eigenvalue problems for partial differential equations
brief.definition: Studies eigenvalues, eigenfunctions, and spectral decompositions of differential operators arising from PDEs, connecting analysis to operator theory.

blocks.name: Applications, Generalized Operators, and Miscellany
blocks.description: Gathers PDEs organized by their origin in mathematical physics and other applied fields, the operator-theoretic generalization of differential operators to pseudodifferential and Fourier-integral operators, and a residual class of topics that resist classification elsewhere in 35-XX. This block is where the classical PDE toolkit meets modern microlocal analysis and concrete physical modeling.

brief.name: Partial differential equations of mathematical physics and other areas of application
brief.definition: Covers named equations arising from physical and applied models — such as Navier–Stokes, Schrödinger, Maxwell, and Einstein equations — studied for their specific physical content.

brief.name: Miscellaneous topics in partial differential equations
brief.definition: Covers PDE topics such as equations on infinite-dimensional spaces, stochastic and fractional PDEs, and other subjects not captured by the other 35-XX subclasses.

brief.name: Pseudodifferential operators and other generalizations of partial differential operators
brief.definition: Studies pseudodifferential, Fourier-integral, and other generalized operators that extend differential operators via microlocal and symbol calculus techniques.

blocks.name: Ergodic and Topological Foundations
blocks.description: The measure-theoretic and topological bedrock of dynamics: how orbits distribute statistically under an invariant measure, and how they behave qualitatively on a bare topological or metric space. Entropy, mixing, recurrence, minimality, and symbolic coding are the shared invariants that classify systems up to isomorphism or conjugacy in these two complementary lenses.

brief.name: Ergodic theory
brief.definition: Studies measure-preserving transformations and flows through invariant measures, mixing properties, entropy, and the statistical long-term behavior of orbits.

brief.name: Topological dynamics
brief.definition: Studies dynamical systems on topological or metric spaces using qualitative invariants such as minimality, transitivity, symbolic coding, and topological entropy, independent of any invariant measure.

blocks.name: Smooth, Hyperbolic, and Low-Dimensional Dynamics
blocks.description: The differentiable-geometric core of the theory: general structural results for diffeomorphisms and flows on manifolds, the stable/unstable manifold machinery of uniformly and non-uniformly hyperbolic systems, and the special, more tractable dynamics of maps on intervals, circles, and surfaces. These strata build on one another, from generic smooth theory down to the concrete low-dimensional models where conjectures are first tested.

brief.name: Smooth dynamical systems: general theory
brief.definition: Develops the general qualitative theory of diffeomorphisms and flows on manifolds, including structural stability, invariant manifolds, and generic dynamical properties.

brief.name: Dynamical systems with hyperbolic behavior
brief.definition: Analyzes uniformly and non-uniformly hyperbolic systems (Anosov, Axiom A, partially hyperbolic) via stable/unstable manifold theory and Lyapunov exponents.

brief.name: Low-dimensional dynamical systems
brief.definition: Treats maps and flows on the interval, circle, and surfaces, including interval maps, rotation numbers, and surface homeomorphisms and diffeomorphisms.

blocks.name: Complex and Arithmetic Dynamics
blocks.description: Iteration theory over number-theoretic and analytic ground fields: holomorphic and meromorphic maps on the complex plane or Riemann surfaces, and rational maps over number fields, p-adic fields, and other non-Archimedean valuations. Julia/Fatou set theory sits alongside height functions and canonical heights, forging deep links to complex analysis and arithmetic geometry respectively.

brief.name: Dynamical systems over complex numbers
brief.definition: Studies the iteration of holomorphic and meromorphic maps, Julia and Fatou sets, and complex analytic dynamics in one and several variables.

brief.name: Arithmetic and non-Archimedean dynamical systems
brief.definition: Studies iteration of rational and polynomial maps over number fields, p-adic fields, and other non-Archimedean valued fields, connecting dynamics to arithmetic geometry.

blocks.name: Bifurcation Theory and Random Dynamical Systems
blocks.description: How the qualitative portrait of a system changes as it is perturbed, either by a deterministic parameter or by persistent noise. Local bifurcations (Hopf, saddle-node) and global ones (homoclinic, heteroclinic) sit beside the cocycle formalism of random dynamical systems, whose random attractors and Lyapunov spectra generalize hyperbolicity to a stochastic setting.

brief.name: Local and nonlocal bifurcation theory for dynamical systems
brief.definition: Studies qualitative changes in a system's phase portrait as parameters vary, covering local bifurcations such as Hopf and saddle-node and global bifurcations such as homoclinic and heteroclinic connections.

brief.name: Random dynamical systems
brief.definition: Studies dynamical systems driven by noise or random parameters, including stochastic flows, random attractors, and Lyapunov exponents for cocycles.

blocks.name: Hamiltonian, Lagrangian, and Infinite-Dimensional Dynamics
blocks.description: The dynamics of conservative and dissipative systems arising from mechanics and PDEs. Finite-dimensional Hamiltonian and Lagrangian dynamics contributes integrability, KAM theory, and symplectic invariants; its infinite-dimensional counterpart extends these tools to integrable PDEs and solitons, while dissipative infinite-dimensional systems study global attractors and inertial manifolds for dissipative PDEs and semigroups.

brief.name: Dynamical aspects of finite-dimensional Hamiltonian and Lagrangian systems
brief.definition: Studies the dynamics of classical mechanical systems with finitely many degrees of freedom, including integrability, KAM theory, and symplectic invariants.

brief.name: Dynamical system aspects of infinite-dimensional Hamiltonian and Lagrangian systems
brief.definition: Extends Hamiltonian dynamical methods to infinite-dimensional systems such as integrable PDEs, solitons, and Backlund transformations.

brief.name: Infinite-dimensional dissipative dynamical systems
brief.definition: Studies attractors and long-time dynamics of dissipative PDEs and infinite-dimensional semigroups, including global attractors and inertial manifolds.

blocks.name: Numerical Methods and Applications of Dynamical Systems
blocks.description: The computational and applied wing of the theory: algorithms and computer-assisted proofs for simulating and rigorously analyzing dynamical systems, and the deployment of dynamical-systems tools across biology, economics, engineering, and physics. This block converts the structural theory of the other blocks into concrete, verifiable, and applicable predictions.

brief.name: Approximation methods and numerical treatment of dynamical systems
brief.definition: Covers numerical schemes, computer-assisted proofs, and computational tools for simulating and analyzing dynamical systems.

brief.name: Applications of dynamical systems
brief.definition: Covers applications of dynamical-systems methods to biology, economics, engineering, physics, and other sciences.

blocks.name: Difference Equations
blocks.description: Studies equations relating the values of an unknown sequence or discrete-variable function at shifted arguments, the natural discrete counterpart of ordinary differential equations. Encompasses linear and nonlinear recurrence relations, existence, stability, periodicity, oscillation, and asymptotic behavior of solutions, with close ties to numerical analysis, discrete dynamical systems, and combinatorics. Excludes dynamic equations on time scales (34N05) and the qualitative dynamical-systems viewpoint proper (37-XX), which are cross-referenced rather than absorbed.

brief.name: Difference equations
brief.definition: The study of equations that relate the values of an unknown sequence or discrete-variable function at different (typically consecutive) points, together with the existence, stability, periodicity, and asymptotic behavior of their solutions.

blocks.name: Functional Equations and Inequalities
blocks.description: Concerns equations and inequalities whose unknown is a function itself, constrained by relations among its values at various, possibly unrelated, arguments — the paradigm case being the Cauchy equation f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y) and its many generalizations. The theory develops existence, uniqueness, regularity, and stability (Hyers–Ulam type) results for such functions, with applications spanning information theory, geometry, and characterizations of elementary and special functions. It borders complex-analytic methods for functional equations in one complex variable (see also 30D05).

brief.name: Functional equations and inequalities
brief.definition: The study of equations and inequalities in which the unknown is a function subject to a relation among its values at several, generally independent, arguments, including questions of existence, regularity, and stability of solutions.

blocks.name: Convergence of Sequences and Series
blocks.description: Studies the classical theory of when infinite sequences, series, products, and integrals converge, diverge, or oscillate, including tests, rearrangement behavior, and the extension of these questions to sequences and series indexed by two or more indices. This is the foundational layer on which all summability theory is built, tracing back to Cauchy and Abel.

brief.name: Convergence and divergence of infinite limiting processes
brief.definition: Studies criteria and behavior governing the convergence or divergence of infinite series, sequences, products, and continued fractions.

brief.name: Multiple sequences and series
brief.definition: Extends convergence theory to double and multiple-indexed sequences and series, addressing iterated versus simultaneous limits and Pringsheim-type convergence.

blocks.name: Summability Methods and Their Theorems
blocks.description: Develops linear and nonlinear methods — such as Cesàro, Abel, Borel, and Hölder means — for assigning generalized sums to divergent series, together with the direct (Abelian) theorems showing convergence implies summability, the Tauberian/inversion theorems recovering convergence from summability plus a side condition, and the sharper notion of absolute or strong summability. This is the classical core of the discipline and underlies applications from Fourier series to divergent asymptotic expansions.

brief.name: General summability methods
brief.definition: Formalizes matrix, integral, and other general transformations that assign generalized sums to divergent sequences or series.

brief.name: Direct theorems on summability
brief.definition: Establishes Abelian-type theorems proving that ordinary convergence of a series implies its summability by a given method, with the same sum.

brief.name: Inversion theorems
brief.definition: Proves Tauberian-type theorems that recover ordinary convergence from summability together with an auxiliary regularity condition.

brief.name: Absolute and strong summability
brief.definition: Studies summability methods strengthened to require absolute convergence or strong (bounded-variation-type) convergence of the transformed sequence.

brief.name: Special methods of summability
brief.definition: Catalogues named summability methods — Cesàro, Abel, Borel, Euler, Nörlund, and Riesz means among them — and their comparative properties.

blocks.name: Functional-Analytic and Abstract Summability
blocks.description: Recasts summability theory in the language of functional analysis, using sequence-space duality, matrix operators on Banach and FK-spaces, and Silverman–Toeplitz-type characterizations to unify classical methods, and further extends summability notions to sequences valued in abstract structures such as topological groups, Banach algebras, and locally compact groups. Reflects the twentieth-century abstraction of the subject beyond scalar series.

brief.name: Functional analytic methods in summability
brief.definition: Applies sequence-space and operator-theoretic techniques, including FK-space and matrix-operator theory, to characterize and unify summability methods.

brief.name: Summability in abstract structures
brief.definition: Generalizes summability theory to sequences and series taking values in abstract algebraic or topological structures such as topological groups and Banach algebras.

blocks.name: Approximation Theory and Expansions
blocks.description: Studies how functions can be approximated by simpler classes — polynomials, rational functions, splines, and other structured families — and quantifies the resulting error in various norms. Encompasses best approximation, interpolation, quadrature-adjacent expansion theory, moduli of smoothness, and the asymptotic behavior of approximating sequences as degree or dimension grows. Foundational to numerical analysis, signal processing, and constructive function theory, linking classical Chebyshev/Weierstrass-type results to modern computational approximation schemes. Excludes complex-domain approximation (30E05, 30E10), trigonometric approximation/interpolation (42A10, 42A15), and numerical approximation proper (65Dxx), which are cross-referenced rather than absorbed.

brief.name: Approximations and expansions
brief.definition: The study of approximating functions by simpler classes (polynomials, rational functions, splines, etc.) and of the associated expansions, error bounds, and convergence behavior.

blocks.name: Classical Fourier Analysis on Euclidean Space
blocks.description: Studies the decomposition and reconstruction of functions on the real line and Euclidean n-space via trigonometric systems — Fourier series and the Fourier transform — together with the operators (conjugate function, singular integrals, maximal functions) that control their convergence and summability. In one variable this theory is classical and largely complete, resting on Calderón–Zygmund and Littlewood–Paley technology; in several variables it remains a driving engine of modern analysis through open problems such as the Bochner–Riesz and restriction conjectures, connected to Kakeya-type geometric questions. It underlies PDE regularity theory, signal processing, and the broader Calderón–Zygmund school of singular integral operators.

brief.name: Harmonic analysis in one variable
brief.definition: Studies Fourier series and the Fourier transform on the real line, including convergence, summability, and conjugate-function theory for one-dimensional trigonometric expansions.

brief.name: Harmonic analysis in several variables
brief.definition: Extends Fourier analysis to Euclidean n-space, covering multiplier and singular-integral operators, maximal functions, and open problems such as the Bochner–Riesz and Fourier restriction conjectures.

blocks.name: Nontrigonometric Harmonic Analysis
blocks.description: Concerns function expansions in bases other than the trigonometric system — general orthogonal series, wavelets, frames, and related special-function expansions — together with their convergence, approximation, and stability properties. It generalizes classical Fourier methods to settings where trigonometric functions are not the natural building blocks, providing tools for multiresolution analysis and time-frequency localization used throughout signal processing and numerical analysis.

brief.name: Nontrigonometric harmonic analysis
brief.definition: Studies expansions with respect to non-trigonometric orthogonal systems, including wavelets and frames, and their convergence and approximation properties.

blocks.name: Abstract Harmonic Analysis
blocks.description: Studies Fourier analysis, convolution, and duality on locally compact groups and homogeneous spaces, generalizing classical Fourier series and integrals via Haar measure and unitary representation theory. It underpins the Pontryagin and Peter-Weyl duality theorems, group algebras, and almost periodic functions, and provides the structural framework connecting representation theory to analysis on both abelian and non-abelian groups.

brief.name: Abstract harmonic analysis
brief.definition: The study of Haar measure, convolution algebras, Fourier and Fourier-Stieltjes transforms, and unitary representations on locally compact groups and homogeneous spaces.

blocks.name: Integral Transforms and Operational Calculus
blocks.description: Studies linear integral transforms — Laplace, Hankel, Mellin, Stieltjes, Hilbert, Z, and their many relatives — that convert differential and integral equations into algebraic or simpler analytic problems on a transformed domain, together with the operational calculus built on such transforms to solve linear ODEs, PDEs, and convolution equations symbolically. Encompasses inversion theory, operational rules (shift, convolution, differentiation-multiplication correspondences), and applications to boundary-value problems, signal processing, and special-function identities. This is the classical toolkit bridging real/complex analysis with applied differential equations, largely codified since Heaviside and Doetsch but still extended with new transform kernels and fractional-calculus variants.

brief.name: Integral transforms, operational calculus
brief.definition: The study of linear integral transforms (Laplace, Hankel, Mellin, Stieltjes, Hilbert, and other kernel-based transforms) and the operational calculus built on them for solving differential, integral, and convolution equations.

blocks.name: Foundations of Linear Integral Equations
blocks.description: Studies equations in which an unknown function occurs linearly under an integral sign combined with a kernel — the bedrock formulation from which the rest of 45-XX branches. Fredholm theory classifies solvability via the Fredholm alternative and resolvent kernels for equations of the first and second kind, and this linear-operator machinery extends directly to coupled systems of several simultaneous equations. One of the most classical and thoroughly settled areas of 20th-century analysis, it underlies potential theory and boundary-integral reformulations of elliptic PDEs.

brief.name: Linear integral equations
brief.definition: Studies integral equations in which the unknown function occurs linearly under the integral sign, the general linear framework underlying 45-XX.

brief.name: Fredholm integral equations
brief.definition: Treats equations with a fixed integration domain and kernel, governed by the Fredholm alternative and resolvent-kernel solvability theory.

brief.name: Systems of linear integral equations
brief.definition: Extends linear and Fredholm theory to coupled systems of several simultaneous integral equations in several unknown functions.

blocks.name: Spectral Theory and Integral Operators
blocks.description: Reformulates integral equations as eigenvalue problems for the associated integral operator, connecting 45-XX to functional and operator theory. Compactness of the kernel yields a discrete spectrum via Riesz–Schauder theory, while the study of integral operators as bounded, compact, or singular maps between function spaces underlies spectral and numerical methods across analysis. This area bridges classical integral-equation theory with operator theory on Banach and Hilbert spaces.

brief.name: Eigenvalue problems for integral equations
brief.definition: Studies eigenvalues and eigenfunctions of integral operators, the integral-equation analogue of Sturm–Liouville spectral theory.

brief.name: Integral operators
brief.definition: Studies integral operators themselves — their boundedness, compactness, and spectral properties as maps between function spaces.

blocks.name: Special Classes: Volterra, Singular, and Nonlinear Equations
blocks.description: Groups integral-equation types distinguished by structural features of the kernel or the equation rather than by linearity alone. Volterra equations, with a variable upper limit of integration, encode causal memory-dependent evolution; singular equations carry non-integrable kernel singularities (Cauchy, Hilbert-type) requiring principal-value regularization; equations built around other named or structured kernels form a further catalogue; and nonlinear integral equations, including Hammerstein and Urysohn types, demand fixed-point and monotonicity methods in place of linear-operator theory. Each class resists a uniform Fredholm treatment and carries its own dedicated solvability toolkit.

brief.name: Volterra integral equations
brief.definition: Studies equations with a variable, rather than fixed, upper limit of integration, modeling causal processes with memory.

brief.name: Singular integral equations
brief.definition: Studies equations whose kernel has a non-integrable singularity, solved via Cauchy principal values and Hilbert-transform techniques.

brief.name: Nonlinear integral equations
brief.definition: Studies equations in which the unknown function appears nonlinearly, including Hammerstein- and Urysohn-type equations, via fixed-point methods.

brief.name: Integral equations with miscellaneous special kernels
brief.definition: Catalogues integral equations distinguished by a specific named or structured kernel not covered by the other 45-XX subdivisions.

blocks.name: Integro-Differential Equations
blocks.description: Studies equations that combine differential operators with integral terms, modeling systems whose evolution depends on both an instantaneous rate of change and accumulated history. Integro-ordinary differential equations couple such memory terms with ODEs, as in viscoelasticity and structured population models; integro-partial differential equations do the same for PDEs, arising in nonlocal diffusion, kinetic theory, and delayed reaction-diffusion systems. Both remain active in applied analysis because the interplay of differentiation and integration defies a single unified existence theory.

brief.name: Integro-ordinary differential equations
brief.definition: Studies ordinary differential equations coupled with an integral memory term acting on the unknown function.

brief.name: Integro-partial differential equations
brief.definition: Studies partial differential equations coupled with an integral term, arising in nonlocal diffusion and kinetic models.

blocks.name: Qualitative, Abstract, and Stochastic Theory of Solutions
blocks.description: Gathers the meta-level questions asked about integral equations once existence of a solution is settled: how the solution behaves qualitatively (stability, boundedness, asymptotics), how it can be approximated by theoretical schemes, how the equation generalizes to abstract Banach- or Hilbert-space-valued settings, how unknown kernels or data can be recovered from observed solutions, and how randomness in the kernel or forcing term propagates to the solution process. These strands range from settled asymptotic theory to genuinely open inverse and stochastic problems.

brief.name: Theoretical approximation of solutions to integral equations
brief.definition: Studies theoretical, as opposed to purely numerical, approximation schemes for solutions of integral equations.

brief.name: Qualitative behavior of solutions to integral equations
brief.definition: Studies stability, boundedness, oscillation, and asymptotic behavior of solutions to integral equations.

brief.name: Abstract integral equations, integral equations in abstract spaces
brief.definition: Extends integral-equation theory to unknowns and kernels valued in abstract Banach or Hilbert spaces.

brief.name: Inverse problems for integral equations
brief.definition: Studies the recovery of an unknown kernel, domain, or forcing term of an integral equation from observed solution data.

brief.name: Random integral equations
brief.definition: Studies integral equations with random kernels, coefficients, or forcing terms, and the resulting stochastic solution processes.

blocks.name: Foundational Linear Spaces
blocks.description: The bedrock structures of functional analysis: vector spaces equipped with a topology, a norm, or an inner product, and the geometric and algebraic theory that follows from each level of extra structure. Banach and Hilbert space theory built here — completeness, duality, convexity, orthogonality — underlies essentially every other block in 46-XX and much of operator theory, PDE, and analysis at large. This area is classical and forms the standard graduate curriculum in functional analysis.

brief.name: Topological linear spaces and related structures
brief.definition: Studies vector spaces endowed with a topology compatible with the linear operations, including locally convex, barrelled, and Fréchet spaces, generalizing normed spaces to settings without a single norm.

brief.name: Normed linear spaces and Banach spaces; Banach lattices
brief.definition: Develops the geometry and structure theory of normed and complete (Banach) spaces, including basis theory, convexity, reflexivity, and the ordered-lattice refinements known as Banach lattices.

brief.name: Inner product spaces and their generalizations, Hilbert spaces
brief.definition: Studies spaces equipped with an inner product and their completions, Hilbert spaces, exploiting orthogonality, projection, and self-duality to obtain the sharpest structural results in the normed hierarchy.

blocks.name: Function Spaces, Distributions, and Infinite-Dimensional Analysis
blocks.description: Concrete realizations of abstract linear-space theory as spaces of functions, together with their duals and the generalized-function (distribution) framework needed to differentiate and transform objects that are not classically smooth. Also covers measure, integration, differentiation, and holomorphy carried out directly in infinite-dimensional spaces, connecting functional analysis to harmonic analysis, PDE, and infinite-dimensional measure theory. This is mature core theory with active extensions at its measure-theoretic and holomorphic edges.

brief.name: Linear function spaces and their duals
brief.definition: Studies concrete Banach or locally convex spaces whose elements are functions (Lp, Sobolev, Orlicz, etc.) together with their dual spaces and duality pairings.

brief.name: Distributions, generalized functions, distribution spaces
brief.definition: Develops the theory of distributions and other generalized functions as continuous linear functionals on test-function spaces, extending differentiation and Fourier analysis beyond classical functions.

brief.name: Measures, integration, derivative, holomorphy (all involving infinite-dimensional spaces)
brief.definition: Extends measure theory, integration, differential calculus, and holomorphic function theory to functions and measures defined on infinite-dimensional vector spaces.

blocks.name: Topological and Operator Algebras
blocks.description: The theory of algebras carrying a compatible topology or norm, ranging from general topological and Banach algebras through commutative and involutive refinements to the self-adjoint operator algebras — C*-algebras and von Neumann algebras — that model observables in quantum physics and underlie noncommutative geometry. This progression from general to highly structured algebras is one of the deepest and most productive strands of 20th- and 21st-century analysis, with C*- and von Neumann algebra classification remaining an active research frontier.

brief.name: Topological algebras, normed rings and algebras, Banach algebras
brief.definition: Studies algebras equipped with a compatible topology or norm, in particular complete normed (Banach) algebras, developing spectral theory in an algebraic setting.

brief.name: Commutative Banach algebras and commutative topological algebras
brief.definition: Specializes Banach and topological algebra theory to the commutative case, where Gelfand theory represents elements as functions on the algebra's maximal ideal space.

brief.name: Topological (rings and) algebras with an involution
brief.definition: Studies topological algebras equipped with an involutive anti-automorphism (a *-operation), the structural prerequisite for C*-algebra and Banach *-algebra theory.

brief.name: Selfadjoint operator algebras (C*-algebras, von Neumann (W*-) algebras, etc.)
brief.definition: Studies norm-closed or weakly-closed self-adjoint algebras of bounded operators on Hilbert space, the operator-algebraic framework underlying quantum theory and noncommutative geometry.

blocks.name: Generalized, Nonlinear, and Applied Methods
blocks.description: Cross-cutting extensions of functional analysis beyond the linear, classical core: categorical reformulations of the field's basic constructions, applications to other mathematical and physical domains, nonclassical variants adapted to non-Archimedean or other exotic settings, and genuinely nonlinear functional analysis needed for nonlinear PDE and fixed-point theory. These strands are comparatively young and less unified than the classical blocks, with nonlinear functional analysis in particular remaining an active research frontier tied closely to PDE and variational methods.

brief.name: Methods of category theory in functional analysis
brief.definition: Applies categorical constructions — functors, limits, tensor products — to organize and unify functional-analytic structures and their morphisms.

brief.name: Miscellaneous applications of functional analysis
brief.definition: Covers applications of functional-analytic methods to problems in other areas of mathematics, physics, and engineering not classified elsewhere.

brief.name: Other (nonclassical) types of functional analysis
brief.definition: Encompasses nonclassical variants of functional analysis, such as analysis over non-Archimedean or non-standard fields, that fall outside the classical real/complex framework.

brief.name: Nonlinear functional analysis
brief.definition: Studies nonlinear maps and operators between infinite-dimensional spaces, including differential calculus in Banach spaces and nonlinear fixed-point and variational methods.

blocks.name: Foundations and Algebraic Structures of Linear Operators
blocks.description: Covers the general axiomatic theory of bounded and unbounded linear operators on Banach and Hilbert spaces, the classification of distinguished special classes (self-adjoint, normal, compact, Fredholm, subnormal), the study of individual operators as elements of rings, algebras, and other algebraic systems, and the linear spaces and algebras (operator ideals, C*- and von Neumann-algebra-adjacent structures) they generate. This is the settled, textbook core of operator theory from which every specialized branch below draws its vocabulary — spectral theory, functional calculus, and operator-ideal theory are all rooted here.

brief.name: General theory of linear operators
brief.definition: Develops the foundational spectral, functional-calculus, and structural theory of bounded and unbounded linear operators on Banach and Hilbert spaces.

brief.name: Special classes of linear operators
brief.definition: Classifies distinguished operator families — self-adjoint, normal, compact, Fredholm, Toeplitz, composition, and related classes — by their defining structural and spectral properties.

brief.name: Individual linear operators as elements of algebraic systems
brief.definition: Studies single operators viewed as elements of rings, algebras, or other algebraic structures, emphasizing their algebraic rather than purely analytic behavior.

brief.name: Linear spaces and algebras of operators
brief.definition: Investigates operator ideals, operator algebras, and other linear spaces of operators as structured objects in their own right, including their norms and representations.

blocks.name: Differential, Integral, and Semigroup Operators
blocks.description: Groups the operator-theoretic study of ordinary and partial differential operators, integral and pseudodifferential operators, and the one-parameter groups and semigroups of linear operators that model their associated evolution equations. This area is the analytic bridge between abstract operator theory and PDE, harmonic analysis, and mathematical physics, underpinning existence, regularity, and long-time-behavior results for evolution processes. Semigroup generation theorems and symbol calculus techniques remain active tools under continual refinement.

brief.name: Groups and semigroups of linear operators, their generalizations and applications
brief.definition: Studies one-parameter (and generalized) groups and semigroups of operators, notably C0-semigroups, and their use in solving abstract evolution equations.

brief.name: Ordinary differential operators
brief.definition: Analyzes ordinary differential operators as linear operators, including their spectral theory, self-adjoint extensions, and boundary-value structure.

brief.name: Partial differential operators
brief.definition: Treats partial differential operators through the lens of operator theory, addressing their domains, spectra, and well-posedness as unbounded operators.

brief.name: Integral, integro-differential, and pseudodifferential operators
brief.definition: Covers integral, integro-differential, and pseudodifferential operators, including their symbol calculus, boundedness, and compactness properties.

blocks.name: Nonlinear Operator Theory, Equations, and Applications
blocks.description: Encompasses nonlinear operators and their intrinsic properties, the equations and inequalities they generate (variational inequalities, fixed-point problems), the diverse applications of operator theory across analysis and mathematical physics, and nonclassical operator-theoretic frameworks that extend beyond the linear, classical setting. This cluster sits at the research frontier where monotonicity methods, fixed-point theory, and generalized operator structures (e.g., non-self-adjoint, quantum, or Banach-algebra-external settings) are still being actively developed and unified.

brief.name: Nonlinear operators and their properties
brief.definition: Studies nonlinear operators on normed or topological spaces, including monotonicity, accretivity, continuity, and fixed-point properties.

brief.name: Equations and inequalities involving nonlinear operators
brief.definition: Analyzes equations and variational inequalities defined by nonlinear operators, including existence, uniqueness, and iterative solution methods.

brief.name: Miscellaneous applications of operator theory
brief.definition: Collects applications of operator-theoretic methods to problems in other areas of analysis, mathematical physics, and applied mathematics.

brief.name: Other (nonclassical) types of operator theory
brief.definition: Covers nonclassical operator-theoretic frameworks that fall outside the standard linear or nonlinear taxonomy, including emerging generalized settings.

pillars.name: Calculus of variations and optimal control; optimization

blocks.name: Foundations of Variational and Optimal Control Theory
blocks.description: Covers the core analytic machinery of the calculus of variations and optimal control: proving that minimizers or optimal trajectories exist, characterizing them via first- and second-order optimality conditions (Euler-Lagrange, Pontryagin maximum principle, transversality), propagating value functions through Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman theory, and computing solutions numerically when closed forms are unavailable. This cluster is the backbone that every applied variational problem — from mechanics to economics to engineering control — ultimately draws on. The theory is classical in its core (direct methods, convex duality, dynamic programming) but remains an active site for extensions to nonsmooth, stochastic, and infinite-dimensional settings.

brief.name: Existence theories in calculus of variations and optimal control
brief.definition: Studies conditions — coercivity, lower semicontinuity, compactness, convexity — under which minimizers of variational functionals or optimal controls actually exist.

brief.name: Optimality conditions
brief.definition: Derives necessary and sufficient first- and second-order conditions, such as the Euler-Lagrange equations and the Pontryagin maximum principle, that characterize minimizers and optimal trajectories.

brief.name: Hamilton-Jacobi theories
brief.definition: Develops the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman approach to optimal control and the calculus of variations, including viscosity-solution theory for the associated first-order PDEs.

brief.name: Numerical methods in optimal control
brief.definition: Constructs and analyzes discretization, direct/indirect, and dynamic-programming algorithms for computing approximate solutions to optimal control and variational problems.

brief.name: Miscellaneous topics in calculus of variations and optimal control
brief.definition: Collects auxiliary and cross-cutting topics in calculus of variations and optimal control not captured by the other 49-XX subclasses, such as duality, sensitivity, and inverse problems.

blocks.name: Geometric and Physical Variational Methods
blocks.description: Concerns variational structures tied to geometry, spectral theory, and physics: minimization problems posed on manifolds or via geometric measure theory (minimal surfaces, currents, sets of finite perimeter), variational characterizations of eigenvalues of differential and integral operators, and the least-action and stationary-action principles underlying classical and continuum physics. These subclasses sit at the interface of 49-XX with differential geometry, spectral analysis, and mathematical physics, and each MSC entry explicitly requires pairing with another 49-XX code, reflecting their role as specialized lenses on the core theory rather than self-contained subfields. Shape optimization, spectral gap estimates, and geometric flows keep this area an active research frontier.

brief.name: Manifolds and measure-geometric topics
brief.definition: Treats variational problems formulated on manifolds and via geometric measure theory, including minimal surfaces, currents, and shape optimization.

brief.name: Variational methods for eigenvalues of operators
brief.definition: Uses Rayleigh quotients and min-max variational characterizations to study and estimate eigenvalues of differential and other operators.

brief.name: Variational principles of physics
brief.definition: Formalizes least-action and other stationary-action principles that derive the equations of motion of physical systems from variational functionals.

blocks.name: Incidence Geometry and Axiomatic Foundations
blocks.description: Studies geometric structures defined purely by incidence relations among points, lines, and higher flats, abstracted from classical affine and projective geometry through synthetic axiom systems in the tradition of Hilbert's Grundlagen. Encompasses generalizations to non-Desarguesian and non-Pappian planes, coordinatization by rings rather than fields (Hjelmslev and Barbilian planes), and finite combinatorial incidence structures such as generalized polygons, nets, and Steiner systems. Incidence groups tie these axiomatic structures to permutation-group and topological-group actions on the underlying point sets. Together these subclasses form the synthetic combinatorial core of foundational geometry.

brief.name: Linear incidence geometry
brief.definition: Studies affine and projective planes and spaces axiomatized by linear incidence and parallelism relations among points and lines.

brief.name: Nonlinear incidence geometry
brief.definition: Extends incidence-geometric axiomatics to structures whose lines or blocks need not behave linearly, including generalized polygons and near-geometries.

brief.name: Ring geometry (Hjelmslev, Barbilian, etc.)
brief.definition: Develops geometries coordinatized over rings rather than fields, such as Hjelmslev planes and Barbilian spaces, where incidence can fail uniqueness.

brief.name: Finite geometry and special incidence structures
brief.definition: Classifies finite projective and affine planes, generalized quadrangles, and combinatorial designs arising from finite incidence structures.

brief.name: Incidence groups
brief.definition: Studies groups acting on incidence structures and the algebraic structures induced on point sets by such group actions.

blocks.name: Ordered and Closure Geometric Structures
blocks.description: Investigates incidence structures enriched with an order or betweenness relation, and abstract closure operators that capture spanning or convex-hull behavior independently of coordinates. Ordered geometries axiomatize the notion of 'betweenness' underlying real affine geometry without invoking metric or field structure, while geometric closure systems formalize dependency via matroid-like axioms. Geometric order structures extend these convexity ideas toward links with differential-geometric order and causal structures. This is a narrow but foundational corner bridging combinatorics, order theory, and geometry.

brief.name: Geometric closure systems
brief.definition: Axiomatizes spanning and dependency in geometric structures via closure operators generalizing linear and matroid dependence.

brief.name: Ordered geometries (ordered incidence structures, etc.)
brief.definition: Studies incidence structures equipped with a betweenness or order relation that axiomatizes affine order without reference to a metric.

brief.name: Geometric order structures
brief.definition: Examines order structures on geometric spaces generalizing betweenness, with connections to causal and convexity structures in differential geometry.

blocks.name: Metric, Topological, and Distance Geometry
blocks.description: Studies geometric structures equipped with a distance function or a compatible topology, generalizing Euclidean and non-Euclidean metric spaces beyond a purely axiomatic incidence framework. Distance geometry addresses reconstructing point configurations from prescribed pairwise distances, a problem central to rigidity theory, molecular conformation, and sensor-network localization. Topological geometry studies incidence structures whose point and line sets carry compatible topologies, as in topological projective planes and generalized polygons. The distance-geometry strand in particular is driven by active applied and algorithmic research.

brief.name: Metric geometry
brief.definition: Studies geometries axiomatized by a distance function satisfying metric-space or generalized-metric axioms compatible with incidence.

brief.name: Topological geometry
brief.definition: Studies incidence structures such as projective and affine planes whose point and line sets carry topologies compatible with incidence.

brief.name: Distance geometry
brief.definition: Studies the reconstruction and rigidity of point configurations from prescribed pairwise distances.

blocks.name: Real, Complex, Analytic, and Applied Classical Geometry
blocks.description: Covers geometry over the real and complex fields treated by elementary and synthetic methods, including hyperbolic and elliptic non-Euclidean geometry, alongside coordinate-based analytic and descriptive geometry historically used for technical drawing and projection. Classical or axiomatic geometry and physics connects synthetic geometric reasoning to physical theories such as mechanics and relativity, requiring cross-classification with the relevant physics sections. This block bridges pure synthetic geometry with concrete computation and its historical role in engineering, drafting, and physics.

brief.name: Real and complex geometry
brief.definition: Treats elementary and synthetic geometry over the real and complex numbers, including hyperbolic, elliptic, and other non-Euclidean geometries.

brief.name: Analytic and descriptive geometry
brief.definition: Studies geometry via coordinate methods and projective drawing techniques used for technical representation and descriptive construction.

brief.name: Classical or axiomatic geometry and physics
brief.definition: Applies classical or axiomatic geometric methods to physical theories such as mechanics and relativity, requiring joint classification with physics sections.

blocks.name: Convex Bodies and Polytopes
blocks.description: Studies the geometry of convex sets in real vector spaces — their metric, affine, and combinatorial invariants (volume, mixed volumes, support functions, Minkowski sums) — and specializes to polytopes and polyhedra, the finitely generated convex bodies whose face lattices, f-vectors, and combinatorial types organize an entire branch of discrete mathematics. This area underlies convex analysis, the Brunn-Minkowski theory, linear and integer programming geometry, and the classification of regular and semiregular solids. Classical results (Minkowski's theorems, Euler's formula, the Krein-Milman theorem) sit alongside active questions on extremal volumes, rigidity, and realizability of abstract polytopes.

brief.name: General convexity
brief.definition: Studies convex sets and convex bodies in linear, affine, or metric spaces, including their support functions, mixed volumes, extremal problems, and inequalities such as Brunn-Minkowski.

brief.name: Polytopes and polyhedra
brief.definition: Studies polytopes and polyhedra as combinatorial and geometric objects, analyzing their face lattices, f-vectors, symmetry groups, and realizability, including regular, convex, and abstract polytopes.

blocks.name: Discrete and Combinatorial Geometry
blocks.description: Investigates combinatorial properties of finite or discrete configurations of geometric objects — points, lines, circles, convex bodies — including incidence structures, arrangements, packings, coverings, and tilings. Central questions concern extremal counts (unit distances, incidences, k-sets), density bounds for sphere packing and lattice covering, and Helly-type and combinatorial convexity theorems. The area blends geometry with combinatorics and has strong ties to computational geometry, coding theory, and additive combinatorics.

brief.name: Discrete geometry
brief.definition: Studies combinatorial and metric properties of discrete configurations of points, lines, and convex bodies, including packings, coverings, tilings, arrangements, and incidence problems.

blocks.name: Riemannian, Classical & Symplectic-Contact Geometry
blocks.description: Studies the geometry of curves, surfaces, and manifolds equipped with a metric, connection, symplectic form, or contact structure, spanning the classical differential geometry of curves and surfaces through the global curvature-topology interplay of Riemannian and pseudo-Riemannian manifolds and the phase-space geometry underlying Hamiltonian and contact mechanics. It furnishes the foundational language used throughout modern geometry and mathematical physics: Frenet frames, connections, curvature tensors, geodesics, and symplectic and contact forms. Comparison theorems, holonomy classification, and symplectic and contact topology remain active research fronts atop a mature classical core.

brief.name: Classical differential geometry
brief.definition: Studies curves and surfaces in Euclidean, affine, and projective space using classical tools such as Frenet frames and the first and second fundamental forms.

brief.name: Local differential geometry
brief.definition: Investigates the local structure of manifolds carrying a connection, metric, or other differential-geometric structure near a point.

brief.name: Global differential geometry
brief.definition: Examines the global relationship between curvature and topology of Riemannian and pseudo-Riemannian manifolds through geodesics, comparison theorems, and holonomy.

brief.name: Symplectic geometry, contact geometry
brief.definition: Studies manifolds equipped with symplectic, Poisson, or contact structures, the geometric setting for Hamiltonian and contact mechanics.

blocks.name: Geometric Evolution Equations & Applications
blocks.description: Covers partial differential equations that deform a geometric object such as a curve, surface, or Riemannian metric along a flow driven by curvature, including Ricci flow, mean curvature flow, and curve-shortening flow, together with the transfer of differential-geometric methods to the sciences and engineering. Geometric flows analyze long-time behavior and singularity formation, and use flow-based techniques to classify manifolds or optimize shapes, most famously in the resolution of the Poincare and geometrization conjectures. Applications extend these curvature and shape-analysis tools to physics, biology, image processing, and computer vision.

brief.name: Geometric evolution equations
brief.definition: Studies curvature-driven PDE flows of geometric objects, such as Ricci flow and mean curvature flow, and their long-time and singularity behavior.

brief.name: Applications of differential geometry to sciences and engineering
brief.definition: Applies differential-geometric concepts and methods to problems in physics, biology, engineering, and related applied fields.

blocks.name: Foundations and Basic Constructions
blocks.description: Establishes the primitive vocabulary of topology — open sets, convergence of nets and filters, comparison of topologies — and the standard set-theoretic operations by which new spaces are built from old, such as subspaces, products, quotients, and hyperspaces. This is the settled bedrock on which every other area of general topology depends.

brief.name: Generalities in topology
brief.definition: Studies the foundational axioms and basic notions of topological spaces, including comparison of topologies and convergence of nets and filters.

brief.name: Basic constructions in general topology
brief.definition: Develops the standard operations—subspaces, products, quotients, disjoint sums, and hyperspaces—used to build new topological spaces from given ones.

blocks.name: Maps and Classification of Topological Spaces
blocks.description: Studies how continuous, open, closed, and perfect maps shape and relate topological spaces, and organizes spaces both by broad structural invariants (separation axioms, compactness and connectedness variants, covering properties) and by finer special properties such as dimension and orderability. This classificatory core of general topology is classical textbook material, largely settled since the mid-20th century.

brief.name: Maps and general types of topological spaces defined by maps
brief.definition: Classifies topological spaces via distinguished classes of continuous, open, closed, perfect, and quotient maps, such as images of metric or compact spaces.

brief.name: Fairly general properties of topological spaces
brief.definition: Examines coarse structural properties—separation axioms, compactness-type conditions, connectedness, and covering properties—used to classify spaces broadly.

brief.name: Special properties of topological spaces
brief.definition: Treats finer structural invariants, including dimension theory, continua theory, and orderability, that distinguish narrower classes of spaces.

blocks.name: Enriched Structures, Exotic Spaces, and Applications
blocks.description: Enriches bare topological spaces with auxiliary structures such as uniformities, proximities, and metrics; catalogues counterexamples and pathological spaces that mark the boundaries of general theorems; and connects topology to algebra, order theory, dynamics, and nonstandard analysis. The uniform-structure and counterexample theory is classical, while the study of applications and infinitesimal (nonstandard) reformulations remains an active, comparatively niche frontier.

brief.name: Topological spaces with richer structures
brief.definition: Studies spaces endowed with auxiliary structures—metrics, uniformities, proximities, and quasi-uniformities—that refine purely topological data.

brief.name: Peculiar topological spaces
brief.definition: Catalogues counterexamples and pathological spaces exhibiting unusual combinations of topological properties, delimiting the scope of general theorems.

brief.name: Connections of general topology with other structures, applications
brief.definition: Explores interactions between topology and algebraic, order-theoretic, and dynamical structures, including topological groups and topological dynamics, and applications beyond topology proper.

brief.name: Nonstandard topology
brief.definition: Applies nonstandard analysis and infinitesimal methods to reformulate topological notions such as convergence, compactness, and continuity.

blocks.name: Homology, Homotopy, and Classical Invariants
blocks.description: Covers the core invariants of algebraic topology: singular, simplicial, and generalized (co)homology theories, homotopy groups and homotopy types, and the classical fixed-point, degree, and duality theorems built from them. These tools assign algebraic invariants — groups, rings, and homotopy classes of maps — to topological spaces in a way that is functorial and computable, forming the load-bearing machinery used throughout geometry, manifold theory, and mathematical physics. The subject is largely mature, resting on well-established axiomatic frameworks (Eilenberg-Steenrod, model categories), even as specific computations (e.g. stable homotopy groups of spheres) remain hard open problems.

brief.name: Classical topics in algebraic topology
brief.definition: Studies classical invariants and theorems such as fixed-point theory, degree theory, duality, and covering-space arguments applied to topological spaces.

brief.name: Homology and cohomology theories in algebraic topology
brief.definition: Develops singular, simplicial, Čech, and generalized (co)homology theories, together with the axiomatic and computational frameworks that relate them.

brief.name: Homotopy theory
brief.definition: Studies spaces and maps up to homotopy equivalence via CW complexes, model categories, localization, and the classification of homotopy types.

brief.name: Homotopy groups
brief.definition: Computes and organizes homotopy groups of spaces, including stable homotopy groups of spheres and homotopy groups of classifying spaces and Lie groups.

blocks.name: Bundles, Operations, and Homological Machinery
blocks.description: Assembles the structural and computational apparatus that operates on top of homotopy and homology: fiber bundles and their classifying spaces, cohomology operations and obstruction theory, spectral sequences, and the categorical/homological-algebra framework (derived categories, model structures, operads) underlying modern algebraic topology. These tools let topologists extract fine invariants from complex fibered or filtered spaces and organize large computations that would otherwise be intractable. Spectral sequences and higher categorical methods remain an active frontier, continually extended to new settings such as motivic and equivariant homotopy theory.

brief.name: Fiber spaces and bundles in algebraic topology
brief.definition: Studies fiber bundles, fibrations, and their classifying spaces, including characteristic classes that measure their twisting.

brief.name: Operations and obstructions in algebraic topology
brief.definition: Develops cohomology operations (such as Steenrod squares) and obstruction theory for extending or lifting maps across cell complexes.

brief.name: Spectral sequences in algebraic topology
brief.definition: Constructs and applies spectral sequences (Serre, Adams, Atiyah-Hirzebruch, etc.) as filtered computational tools for homology and homotopy.

brief.name: Applied homological algebra and category theory in algebraic topology
brief.definition: Applies simplicial sets, model categories, derived functors, and operadic structures as the categorical foundation for homotopical algebra.

blocks.name: Low-Dimensional Topology
blocks.description: Studies the classification and structure of manifolds in dimensions two through four, where the tools of surgery theory and general position fail and phenomena diverge sharply from the high-dimensional world. Central objects include knots, links, surfaces, and 3- and 4-manifolds, studied through combinatorial, geometric, and increasingly gauge-theoretic and Floer-theoretic invariants. The 3-dimensional case was largely resolved by Thurston's geometrization program and Perelman's proof of the Poincaré conjecture; dimension four remains wide open.

brief.name: Low-dimensional topology in specific dimensions
brief.definition: Studies knots, links, and 3- and 4-manifolds via dimension-specific invariants such as Jones polynomials, Khovanov homology, and Heegaard Floer theory.

brief.name: General low-dimensional topology
brief.definition: Develops general combinatorial and group-theoretic techniques for surfaces and 3-manifolds, including fundamental groups, covering spaces, and Heegaard splittings.

blocks.name: Manifold Structures and PL-Topology
blocks.description: Compares the principal categories in which manifolds can be studied — topological (up to homeomorphism), piecewise-linear (up to PL-homeomorphism), and generalized homology manifolds — and asks when structures in one category can be found, classified, or triangulated in another. Surgery theory, the Hauptvermutung, and the triangulation conjecture (resolved negatively by Manolescu in 2013 using Pin(2)-equivariant Seiberg-Witten theory) are the organizing themes. The classical comparison results are settled; generalized-manifold theory retains active corners.

brief.name: Topological manifolds
brief.definition: Classifies manifolds up to homeomorphism and studies their embeddings and characterizations, chiefly via surgery theory and the Hauptvermutung.

brief.name: Generalized manifolds
brief.definition: Studies spaces such as ANR homology manifolds that share the local cohomological properties of manifolds without necessarily being genuine manifolds.

brief.name: PL-topology
brief.definition: Studies manifolds and complexes with a piecewise-linear structure, including triangulations, PL classification, and their relation to the smooth and topological categories.

blocks.name: Differential Topology and Transformation Groups
blocks.description: Studies smooth manifold structures — exotic smoothings, cobordism, and characteristic classes — alongside the groups and homotopy-theoretic structures that act on or model manifolds. Group actions by diffeomorphisms or homeomorphisms, their fixed-point sets and quotients, and the homology and homotopy theory of the acting Lie or topological groups are treated as a unified package, since transformation-group problems routinely reduce to differential-topological ones and vice versa. Classical cobordism and characteristic-class theory are mature, but exotic smooth structures in dimension four remain a leading open frontier.

brief.name: Differential topology
brief.definition: Studies differentiable manifolds up to diffeomorphism, including exotic smooth structures, cobordism theory, characteristic classes, and smoothing theory.

brief.name: Topological transformation groups
brief.definition: Studies group actions on manifolds by homeomorphisms or diffeomorphisms, together with their classification, fixed-point sets, and orbit spaces.

brief.name: Homology and homotopy of topological groups and related structures
brief.definition: Studies the homology and homotopy theory of topological and Lie groups, homogeneous spaces, and their classifying spaces.

blocks.name: Manifolds and Topology in Science and Engineering
blocks.description: Applies manifold theory, cell complexes, and low-dimensional topology to problems arising outside pure mathematics, including topological data analysis, configuration spaces in robotics, and knot-theoretic models in physics and molecular biology. This is a young interface field where classical topological invariants are repurposed as practical descriptors of shape, connectivity, and constraint in applied data.

brief.name: Relations of manifolds and cell complexes with science and engineering
brief.definition: Covers applications of manifold and cell-complex theory to physics, engineering, robotics, and data science, including topological data analysis and configuration-space models.

blocks.name: Foundations: Manifolds, Infinite-Dimensional Spaces, and General Structures
blocks.description: Establishes the basic differentiable-manifold framework that global analysis builds on, including its extension to infinite-dimensional model spaces (Banach/Frechet manifolds) and the generalized geometric structures - pseudogroups, groupoids, foliation-type data - that go beyond the classical finite-dimensional Lie-group-acted manifold.
