This layer supplies the ambient objects on which calculus, operator theory, and PDE are subsequently developed. It sits at the interface with differential topology, Lie theory, and foliation theory.

brief.name: General theory of differentiable manifolds
brief.definition: Develops the basic definitions, categories, and structural results for smooth (finite-dimensional) manifolds underlying global analysis.

brief.name: Infinite-dimensional manifolds
brief.definition: Extends manifold theory to Banach, Hilbert, and Frechet model spaces, enabling analysis on function spaces and gauge-theoretic configuration spaces.

brief.name: Pseudogroups, differentiable groupoids and general structures on manifolds
brief.definition: Studies pseudogroups, Lie groupoids, and other generalized transformation structures that encode local symmetries and foliated geometry on manifolds.

blocks.name: Calculus on Manifolds, Mapping Spaces, and Variational Problems
blocks.description: Covers nonlinear analysis intrinsic to manifolds: differential calculus of nonlinear operators, the geometry of spaces and manifolds built from mappings between manifolds (infinite-dimensional analogues of function spaces), and variational problems posed in these infinite-dimensional settings, such as harmonic maps and minimal submanifolds obtained as critical points of geometric functionals. It is the technical engine behind global existence results in geometric analysis, connecting Morse theory, the calculus of variations, and nonlinear functional analysis.

brief.name: Calculus on manifolds; nonlinear operators
brief.definition: Studies differentiation, integration, and nonlinear operator theory (e.g. Fredholm maps, degree theory) for mappings between manifolds.

brief.name: Spaces and manifolds of mappings
brief.definition: Investigates the topology and differentiable structure of spaces of mappings between manifolds, generalizing function-space theory to a nonlinear, infinite-dimensional setting.

brief.name: Variational problems in infinite-dimensional spaces
brief.definition: Analyzes critical points of functionals on infinite-dimensional manifolds, including geodesics, harmonic maps, minimal surfaces, and Morse-theoretic methods.

blocks.name: PDE, Singularity Theory, and Applications of Global Analysis
blocks.description: Addresses partial differential equations and differential operators defined intrinsically on manifolds (elliptic operators, index theory, heat-kernel methods), the classification of singularities of smooth maps via catastrophe theory, and the deployment of these global-analytic tools to problems arising in physics and the other sciences. This block links geometric analysis to spectral theory, bifurcation phenomena, and mathematical physics.

brief.name: Partial differential equations on manifolds; differential operators
brief.definition: Studies elliptic and other differential operators and PDEs formulated intrinsically on manifolds, including index theory and heat-kernel asymptotics.

brief.name: Theory of singularities and catastrophe theory
brief.definition: Classifies singularities of smooth maps and unfoldings of critical points, forming the mathematical basis of catastrophe theory.

brief.name: Applications of global analysis to the sciences
brief.definition: Applies manifold-based analytic techniques to problems in physics and other sciences, such as gauge theory and dynamical modeling.

blocks.name: Foundations, Distribution Theory, and Limit Theorems
blocks.description: Comprises the axiomatic and analytic core of probability: measure-theoretic foundations, the theory of distributions and characteristic functions, combinatorial probability on discrete structures, and the asymptotic laws — laws of large numbers, central limit theorems, large deviations — governing sums and sequences of random variables. This is the settled bedrock on which every other area of the discipline builds, codified in the classical textbooks of Kolmogorov, Feller, and Billingsley. New results here typically refine rates, sharpen inequalities, or extend classical limits to dependent or high-dimensional settings rather than overturn the framework.

brief.name: Foundations of probability theory
brief.definition: Establishes the measure-theoretic axioms, probability spaces, and general conceptual apparatus underlying the rest of probability theory.

brief.name: Combinatorial probability
brief.definition: Applies probabilistic reasoning to discrete combinatorial structures, including random graphs, permutations, urn schemes, and occupancy problems.

brief.name: Distribution theory
brief.definition: Studies the analytic properties of probability distributions — characteristic functions, infinite divisibility, moments, and distributional inequalities.

brief.name: Limit theorems in probability theory
brief.definition: Characterizes the asymptotic behavior of sequences and sums of random variables via laws of large numbers, central limit theorems, and large-deviation principles.

blocks.name: Probability on Structured and Geometric Spaces
blocks.description: Extends probability beyond the real line to random elements valued in topological groups, Banach spaces, and other algebraic structures, and to geometric probability, which studies random points, sets, and shapes embedded in Euclidean space or on manifolds. Random walks on groups, invariance principles in infinite dimensions, and stochastic geometry's random tessellations and point processes tie analysis, algebra, and geometry into a common probabilistic language. The area sits at an active interface with harmonic analysis and integral geometry, with steady but not explosive growth.

brief.name: Probability theory on algebraic and topological structures
brief.definition: Studies probability measures and random elements on groups, vector spaces, and other algebraic or topological structures, including weak convergence in such settings.

brief.name: Geometric probability and stochastic geometry
brief.definition: Analyzes random geometric objects — points, sets, lines, and tessellations — and the probabilistic laws governing their configuration and interaction.

blocks.name: Stochastic Processes, Markov Theory, and Special Models
blocks.description: Develops the general theory of stochastic processes — martingales, Gaussian and stationary processes, stopping times — together with Markov processes, whose transition structure links probability to potential theory and partial differential equations, and the special process families (branching, renewal, queueing) that model discrete stochastic evolution in applications. Markov process theory remains a deep and actively researched bridge between probability and analysis, while renewal and queueing theory continue to drive applied stochastic modeling in operations research and biology.

brief.name: Stochastic processes
brief.definition: Provides the general theory of families of random variables indexed by time or space, covering martingales, Gaussian processes, and stationarity.

brief.name: Markov processes
brief.definition: Studies processes whose future evolution depends on the present state alone, including Markov chains, diffusions, and their transition semigroups.

brief.name: Special processes
brief.definition: Covers specialized stochastic process models arising in applications, such as renewal processes, queueing systems, and branching processes.

blocks.name: Stochastic Calculus and Rough Analysis
blocks.description: Concerns the calculus built atop stochastic processes: Itô and Malliavin stochastic calculus, stochastic differential and partial differential equations, and the pathwise rough-path and regularity-structures framework that gives meaning to equations driven by highly irregular noise. Stochastic analysis is a mature, deeply developed field underpinning mathematical finance and SPDE theory, while rough analysis, launched by Lyons in the late 1990s and extended by Hairer's regularity structures, remains a vigorous open research frontier pushing beyond the classical semimartingale setting.

brief.name: Stochastic analysis
brief.definition: Develops stochastic calculus — stochastic integrals, differential equations, and Malliavin calculus — for processes driven by Brownian motion and related noises.

brief.name: Rough analysis
brief.definition: Constructs a pathwise theory of differential equations driven by irregular signals via rough paths and regularity structures, independent of probabilistic averaging.

blocks.name: Foundations, Information, and Decision Theory
blocks.description: Establishes the conceptual and mathematical bedrock of statistical inference: the axiomatic and philosophical foundations of the subject, the information-theoretic concepts (sufficiency, ancillarity, Fisher information) that measure what data reveal about unknown parameters, and the decision-theoretic framework (risk, admissibility, minimaxity, Bayes rules) used to evaluate and choose among statistical procedures.

brief.name: Foundational topics in statistics
brief.definition: Examines the axiomatic, philosophical, and conceptual foundations underlying statistical reasoning and inference.

brief.name: Sufficiency and information
brief.definition: Studies sufficient and ancillary statistics and information measures such as Fisher information that quantify what data reveal about a parameter.

brief.name: Statistical decision theory
brief.definition: Develops the general theory of decision rules, loss and risk functions, admissibility, and minimax and Bayes procedures for statistical decision-making under uncertainty.

blocks.name: Sampling Theory and Distribution Theory
blocks.description: Covers the probabilistic and design-based machinery underlying statistical modeling: the exact and asymptotic distributions of statistics derived from probability models, and the theory of how observational units are selected from finite or infinite populations via survey sampling designs such as stratified and cluster sampling.

brief.name: Statistical sampling theory and related topics
brief.definition: Develops the theory of survey sampling designs — simple random, stratified, cluster, and related schemes — for drawing representative samples from finite or infinite populations.

brief.name: Statistical distribution theory
brief.definition: Derives the exact and asymptotic probability distributions of statistics and estimators used as the basis for statistical models and inference procedures.

blocks.name: Parametric and Nonparametric Inference
blocks.description: Forms the core estimation-and-testing engine of statistics: parametric inference exploits a probability model known up to a finite-dimensional parameter via maximum likelihood, confidence intervals, and likelihood-ratio tests, while nonparametric inference relaxes distributional assumptions using rank-based, empirical-process, and kernel-smoothing methods.

brief.name: Parametric inference
brief.definition: Provides estimation and hypothesis-testing methods, including maximum likelihood and likelihood-ratio tests, for models specified up to a finite-dimensional parameter.

brief.name: Nonparametric inference
brief.definition: Provides estimation and testing procedures, such as rank-based tests and kernel or empirical-process methods, that avoid strong parametric distributional assumptions.

blocks.name: Multivariate Analysis and Regression
blocks.description: Addresses statistical modeling of relationships among several variables simultaneously: multivariate analysis studies the joint covariance structure of vector-valued observations through tools like principal components, factor analysis, and discriminant analysis, while linear inference builds regression and analysis-of-variance models relating a response to explanatory variables.

brief.name: Multivariate analysis
brief.definition: Studies the joint distribution and covariance structure of vector-valued observations using techniques such as principal component, factor, discriminant, and cluster analysis.

brief.name: Linear inference, regression
brief.definition: Develops the theory of linear models, least-squares regression, and analysis of variance for relating a response variable to one or more explanatory variables.

blocks.name: Experimental Design and Sequential Methods
blocks.description: Concerns how statistical data collection is planned and adaptively controlled: the combinatorial and statistical theory of experimental designs — factorial, block, and optimal designs — that allocate treatments efficiently, together with sequential methods in which the sample size or stopping rule adapts to data observed so far, such as sequential probability ratio tests and optimal stopping.

brief.name: Design of statistical experiments
brief.definition: Develops the combinatorial and statistical theory of experimental designs, including factorial, block, and optimal designs, for allocating treatments to maximize information gain.

brief.name: Sequential statistical methods
brief.definition: Studies statistical procedures, such as sequential probability ratio tests and optimal stopping rules, in which sampling continues adaptively based on data observed so far.

blocks.name: Stochastic-Process and Survival Inference
blocks.description: Extends inference to data with temporal dependence or incomplete observation: inference from stochastic processes covers estimation and testing for time series, Markov chains, and diffusions, while survival analysis handles time-to-event data subject to censoring or truncation via tools such as the Kaplan–Meier estimator and proportional-hazards regression.

brief.name: Inference from stochastic processes
brief.definition: Develops estimation and testing methods for data generated by stochastic processes, including time series, Markov chains, and diffusion models.

brief.name: Survival analysis and censored data
brief.definition: Studies statistical methods for time-to-event data subject to censoring or truncation, including hazard-rate estimation, the Kaplan–Meier estimator, and proportional-hazards models.

blocks.name: Applications and Reference Tables
blocks.description: Gathers the applied and infrastructural periphery of the discipline: statistical methodology developed for and used within specific substantive fields such as biology, medicine, and engineering, together with the standardized numerical tables of critical values and distribution quantiles that practitioners historically consulted to apply statistical tests.

brief.name: Applications of statistics
brief.definition: Encompasses statistical methodology developed for and applied within specific substantive domains such as biology, medicine, engineering, and the social sciences.

brief.name: Statistical tables
brief.definition: Comprises standardized numerical tables of critical values and distribution quantiles used to apply statistical tests and construct confidence intervals.

blocks.name: Statistics on Algebraic and Topological Structures
blocks.description: Extends statistical inference beyond Euclidean vector spaces to data valued on manifolds, shape spaces, trees, networks, and other algebraic or topological structures, encompassing directional statistics, shape analysis, and topological data analysis. This is among the youngest and most actively researched frontiers of the discipline, driven by modern non-Euclidean and structured data.

brief.name: Statistics on algebraic and topological structures
brief.definition: Develops statistical inference for data valued in non-Euclidean spaces such as manifolds, shape spaces, trees, and networks, including directional statistics and topological data analysis.

blocks.name: Foundational Tools, Error Analysis & Computation
blocks.description: Covers the infrastructural and cross-cutting apparatus of numerical analysis: precomputed tables, convergence-acceleration techniques, rigorous error and interval bounds, graphical/nomographic solution methods, and the computational-complexity, parallel, and software aspects shared across all numerical algorithms. These subclasses do not target a single equation type but supply the scaffolding used throughout the discipline.

brief.name: Tables in numerical analysis
brief.definition: Covers the construction, organization, and use of numerical tables of function values and mathematical constants as computational aids predating widespread digital computation.

brief.name: Acceleration of convergence in numerical analysis
brief.definition: Studies extrapolation and sequence-transformation techniques, such as Aitken's Delta-squared, Richardson extrapolation, and Pade approximants, that speed the convergence of slowly converging sequences and series.

brief.name: Error analysis and interval analysis
brief.definition: Quantifies and rigorously bounds rounding, truncation, and propagated error in numerical computation, including interval-arithmetic methods that yield guaranteed enclosures of the true result.

brief.name: Graphical methods in numerical analysis
brief.definition: Covers classical graphical and nomographic techniques historically used to obtain approximate numerical solutions without digital computation.

brief.name: Computer aspects of numerical algorithms
brief.definition: Studies computational complexity, parallel and distributed implementation, and software/hardware performance aspects of numerical algorithms.

blocks.name: Approximation, Geometry, and Transform Methods
blocks.description: Covers the numerical treatment of continuous functions and geometric objects: interpolation, quadrature, spline and computational-geometric algorithms, numerical conformal mapping and potential theory, integral equations and transforms, and Fourier-analytic algorithms such as the fast Fourier transform. These subclasses share the goal of representing, approximating, or transforming continuous mathematical objects computationally.

brief.name: Numerical approximation and computational geometry (primarily algorithms)
brief.definition: Covers algorithms for interpolation, function approximation, numerical differentiation and quadrature, spline construction, and computational-geometric problems such as mesh generation and convex hulls.

brief.name: Numerical methods in complex analysis (potential theory, etc.)
brief.definition: Treats numerical conformal mapping, potential-theory computations, and other algorithmic techniques for functions of a complex variable.

brief.name: Numerical methods for integral equations, integral transforms
brief.definition: Covers discretization and solution algorithms for integral equations, such as Fredholm and Volterra equations, and fast computation of integral transforms.

brief.name: Numerical methods in Fourier analysis
brief.definition: Covers fast Fourier transform algorithms and numerical trigonometric approximation and interpolation techniques.

blocks.name: Algebraic and Variational Solvers
blocks.description: Covers the numerical solution of algebraic, transcendental, and variational problems: linear systems and eigenproblems, nonlinear equation solving, optimization and mathematical programming, and the extension of these methods to operator equations posed in infinite-dimensional abstract spaces. Together these subclasses form the algorithmic core underlying almost every other numerical discipline.

brief.name: Numerical linear algebra
brief.definition: Covers direct and iterative algorithms for solving linear systems, eigenvalue problems, and matrix factorizations, together with their stability and complexity analysis.

brief.name: Nonlinear algebraic or transcendental equations
brief.definition: Develops iterative algorithms, including Newton-type, fixed-point, and homotopy/continuation methods, for solving single and systems of nonlinear equations.

brief.name: Numerical analysis in abstract spaces
brief.definition: Extends numerical methods and error analysis to operator equations posed in Banach, Hilbert, and other infinite-dimensional abstract spaces.

brief.name: Numerical methods for mathematical programming, optimization and variational techniques
brief.definition: Covers algorithms for linear, nonlinear, and combinatorial optimization, variational inequalities, and related mathematical-programming problems.

blocks.name: Differential and Difference Equations
blocks.description: Covers discretization and solution methods for equations describing continuous and discrete evolution: ordinary differential equations, time-dependent partial differential equations, boundary-value partial differential equations, and difference, functional, and recurrence equations. This is the largest thematic cluster in numerical analysis, underpinning simulation across science and engineering.

brief.name: Numerical methods for ordinary differential equations
brief.definition: Develops and analyzes discretization schemes, including Runge-Kutta, multistep, and collocation methods, for initial- and boundary-value problems in ordinary differential equations.

brief.name: Numerical methods for partial differential equations, initial value and time-dependent initial-boundary value problems
brief.definition: Covers finite-difference, finite-element, finite-volume, and spectral schemes for evolutionary, time-dependent partial differential equations.

brief.name: Numerical methods for partial differential equations, boundary value problems
brief.definition: Covers discretization methods, including finite element, finite difference, and boundary element methods, for elliptic and other boundary-value partial differential equations.

brief.name: Numerical methods for difference and functional equations, recurrence relations
brief.definition: Develops numerical algorithms for solving difference equations, functional equations, and recurrence relations.

blocks.name: Stochastic and Dynamical-Systems Methods
blocks.description: Covers numerical treatment of randomness and of long-term qualitative behavior: Monte Carlo and quasi-Monte Carlo methods, numerical schemes for stochastic differential equations, and computational techniques for invariant sets, bifurcations, Lyapunov exponents, and other dynamical-systems phenomena. Both areas trade classical deterministic error analysis for probabilistic or qualitative-behavior guarantees.

brief.name: Probabilistic methods, stochastic differential equations
brief.definition: Develops Monte Carlo, quasi-Monte Carlo, and numerical schemes for stochastic differential equations and other random processes.

brief.name: Numerical problems in dynamical systems
brief.definition: Studies numerical computation of invariant sets, bifurcations, Lyapunov exponents, and the long-term behavior of discrete and continuous dynamical systems.

blocks.name: Applications to the Sciences
blocks.description: Covers numerical-analysis methods developed or adapted for specific scientific and engineering application domains, bridging general algorithmic theory with domain-specific computational practice. It serves as the discipline's designated interface to physics, engineering, and other applied fields.

brief.name: Applications to the sciences
brief.definition: Covers numerical-analysis techniques tailored to specific application domains in physics, engineering, and other sciences.

blocks.name: Computational Theory and Algorithms
blocks.description: Covers the formal foundations of computation: models of what can be computed, the resource costs of computing it, the discrete combinatorial and graph-theoretic structures that computational problems are built from, and the design and rigorous analysis of algorithms that solve them. This area supplies the theoretical backbone shared by every other branch of computer science, from software verification to artificial intelligence. Central open problems, notably P versus NP and its many complexity-class relatives, remain unresolved despite decades of intensive study.

brief.name: Theory of software
brief.definition: Studies formal methods for specifying, semantically defining, and verifying the correctness of programs and programming languages.

brief.name: Theory of computing
brief.definition: Studies abstract models of computation, computability, and computational complexity that bound what algorithms can achieve and at what cost.

brief.name: Discrete mathematics in relation to computer science
brief.definition: Studies the combinatorial, graph-theoretic, and coding-theoretic structures that underpin computational problems and their algorithmic treatment.

brief.name: Algorithms in computer science
brief.definition: Studies the design, correctness proofs, and complexity analysis of sequential, parallel, distributed, and randomized algorithms.

blocks.name: Systems, Data, and Computational Practice
blocks.description: Addresses the mathematical modeling of how computers are organized as physical and networked systems, how data is structured, stored, and retrieved, and how computational techniques are applied to concrete practical and scientific tasks. It also encompasses the software infrastructure, proof assistants, symbolic computation systems, computational libraries, that mathematics itself relies on to conduct and verify research. These subareas are mature engineering-adjacent disciplines with well-established core theory, even as specific technologies continue to evolve.

brief.name: Computer system organization
brief.definition: Studies the architecture, networking, distribution, parallelism, security, and reliability of computer hardware and system-level software.

brief.name: Theory of data
brief.definition: Studies the mathematical organization, storage, retrieval, and structural properties of data, including databases and abstract data types.

brief.name: Computing methodologies and applications
brief.definition: Studies computational techniques applied to tasks such as graphics, image processing, and simulation across scientific and engineering domains.

brief.name: Computer science support for mathematical research and practice
brief.definition: Studies computational tools, proof assistants, and symbolic-computation software built specifically to support mathematical discovery and verification.

blocks.name: Artificial Intelligence
blocks.description: Studies the mathematical foundations of automated reasoning, machine learning, knowledge representation, and intelligent agent behavior. It spans classical symbolic approaches such as logic-based planning and search, alongside statistical and neural-network learning theory, and underlies the current wave of large-scale generative models. The field is a fast-moving research frontier where foundational questions about generalization, learnability, and the theoretical limits of learning remain wide open.

brief.name: Artificial intelligence
brief.definition: Studies the mathematical theory of automated reasoning, learning, knowledge representation, and the design of intelligent agents.

blocks.name: Operations Research and Management Science
blocks.description: Operations research and management science builds quantitative models — queueing systems, inventory control, scheduling, network flows, reliability, and simulation — to guide decision-making in industrial, logistical, and organizational systems. It draws on probability, combinatorics, and applied optimization to analyze the performance and control of complex service and production processes. As a mature interface between mathematics and practice, it continues to expand into new domains such as supply-chain resilience, stochastic service networks, and data-driven operations.

brief.name: Operations research and management science
brief.definition: Covers the mathematical modeling and analysis of operational systems — queueing, inventory, scheduling, reliability, and network flows — to support decisions in industry and management.

blocks.name: Mathematical Programming
blocks.description: Mathematical programming is the theory and algorithmic study of optimizing an objective function over a feasible region defined by equality, inequality, or combinatorial constraints, spanning linear, nonlinear, integer, convex, and stochastic programming. It furnishes the duality theory, complexity analysis, and solution methods — simplex, interior-point, branch-and-bound, cutting-plane, and first-order algorithms — that underlie computational optimization across engineering, economics, and machine learning. Research remains highly active on large-scale, nonconvex, and data-driven optimization, with structural links to variational analysis (49Mxx) and numerical linear algebra (65Kxx).

brief.name: Mathematical programming
brief.definition: Studies the theory, geometry, and algorithms for optimizing an objective function subject to constraints, including linear, nonlinear, integer, convex, and stochastic programming.

pillars.name: Game theory, economics, finance, and other social and behavioral sciences

blocks.name: Game Theory, Mathematical Economics, and Finance
blocks.description: Studies formal models of strategic and economic decision-making: how rational (and boundedly rational) agents compete, cooperate, trade, and price risk. Spans the axiomatic core of game theory, general-equilibrium and welfare theory in economics, and the stochastic-analytic machinery of modern quantitative finance and actuarial risk. These three areas share a common toolkit — optimization, equilibrium concepts, and probabilistic modeling — and constantly cross-fertilize, e.g. auction theory informing market microstructure, or stochastic games informing derivative pricing under strategic frictions.

brief.name: Game theory
brief.definition: Studies mathematical models of strategic interaction among rational agents, covering cooperative and noncooperative games, equilibrium concepts, and extensive- and normal-form representations.

brief.name: Mathematical economics
brief.definition: Develops formal mathematical models of economic behavior, market exchange, general equilibrium, welfare, and growth, distinct from the statistical methodology of econometrics.

brief.name: Actuarial science and mathematical finance
brief.definition: Applies stochastic analysis and probability theory to derivative pricing, risk management, portfolio optimization, and insurance/actuarial modeling.

blocks.name: Mathematical Social and Behavioral Sciences
blocks.description: Encompasses the mathematical formalization of human collective and individual behavior outside the strictly economic sphere: social structure and networks, cognition and perception, cultural and group dynamics, and other social-science domains such as political science and linguistics. These subclasses borrow tools from probability, graph theory, dynamical systems, and measurement theory to build testable models of phenomena that are otherwise studied qualitatively. The field is heterogeneous and less axiomatically unified than game theory or finance, with model choice often driven by the specific empirical domain.

brief.name: Social and behavioral sciences: general topics
brief.definition: Covers general-purpose mathematical and structural models — measurement, networks, and data representations — used across the social and behavioral sciences without being tied to one subfield.

brief.name: Mathematical sociology (including anthropology)
brief.definition: Formalizes social structure, group dynamics, social networks, and cultural transmission using mathematical and computational models of collective behavior.

brief.name: Mathematical psychology
brief.definition: Models cognitive processes, perception, learning, and decision-making through mathematical frameworks, distinct from the statistical methodology of psychometrics.

brief.name: Other social and behavioral sciences (mathematical treatment)
brief.definition: Provides mathematical treatments of social-science domains such as political science, geography, and linguistics that fall outside the more specific 91C–91E subclasses.

blocks.name: Foundations, System Structure, and Model Classes
blocks.description: Establishes the abstract mathematical framework of dynamical and control systems—states, inputs, outputs, and their algebraic or categorical formalization—together with the structural properties (controllability, observability, realization, canonical forms) that determine when a system can be steered or reconstructed from data. It further classifies control systems by model type, spanning linear, nonlinear, time-varying, discrete-time, hybrid, delay, and distributed-parameter formulations. This block underlies nearly all applied control-engineering mathematics, from state-space design to system identification.

brief.name: General systems theory
brief.definition: Develops the abstract mathematical formalism of dynamical systems—states, inputs, outputs, and their transformations—independent of any particular model class.

brief.name: Controllability, observability, and system structure
brief.definition: Studies structural properties of control systems such as controllability, observability, realization theory, canonical forms, and system equivalence or decomposition.

brief.name: Model systems in control theory
brief.definition: Classifies and analyzes control systems according to model type, including linear, nonlinear, time-varying, discrete-time, hybrid, delay, and distributed-parameter systems.

blocks.name: Stability, Robustness, and Stochastic Control
blocks.description: Analyzes the qualitative behavior of feedback systems under perturbation, using Lyapunov-type and input-output methods to certify stability, robustness margins, and stabilizability by feedback design. It extends this analysis to systems driven by random disturbances, encompassing stochastic stability, filtering, estimation, and stochastic or adaptive optimal control. Together these topics govern whether a controlled system behaves reliably in the presence of uncertainty, whether deterministic or probabilistic.

brief.name: Stability of control systems
brief.definition: Investigates Lyapunov and input-output stability, robustness margins, and feedback stabilization of control systems.

brief.name: Stochastic systems and control
brief.definition: Treats control, filtering, and estimation problems for systems subject to random disturbances, including stochastic stability and stochastic optimal control.

blocks.name: Communication and Coding Theory
blocks.description: Covers the mathematical theory of transmitting, compressing, and protecting information: entropy and channel-capacity results in the Shannon tradition, source and channel coding, and the algebraic/combinatorial construction and decoding of error-correcting and error-detecting codes. These two subclasses form the mathematical core of digital communication engineering, underpinning everything from data compression to reliable storage and wireless transmission. Classical linear and cyclic code theory is well settled, while modern topics such as LDPC, polar, and quantum error-correcting codes remain an active research frontier.

brief.name: Communication, information
brief.definition: Studies the mathematical foundations of information transmission, storage, and processing, including entropy, source coding, and channel capacity in the Shannon-theoretic tradition.

brief.name: Theory of error-correcting codes and error-detecting codes
brief.definition: Studies algebraic and combinatorial constructions of codes — block, cyclic, convolutional, and modern sparse-graph codes — together with their decoding algorithms, that protect transmitted or stored data against noise and errors.

blocks.name: Circuits, Networks, and General Information-Theoretic Topics
blocks.description: Groups the graph- and logic-theoretic study of electrical and switching circuits and networks with the residual miscellaneous topics of the discipline, chiefly fuzzy-set and possibility-theoretic generalizations of classical information measures. Circuit and network theory analyzes connectivity, switching, and Boolean-complexity properties of physical and logical networks, closely tied to combinatorics and complexity theory. The miscellaneous subclass captures uncertainty-based and non-probabilistic information frameworks that extend Shannon-style theory beyond its classical probabilistic setting.

brief.name: Circuits, networks
brief.definition: Studies mathematical models of electrical and logical circuits and networks, including graph-theoretic connectivity analysis and Boolean-circuit complexity of switching networks.

brief.name: Miscellaneous topics in information and communication theory
brief.definition: Covers topics in information and communication theory not classified elsewhere in 94-XX, notably fuzzy sets, possibility theory, and other uncertainty-based generalizations of classical information measures.

blocks.name: Foundations, Policy, and Research in Mathematics Education
blocks.description: Encompasses the historical, social, institutional, and psychological dimensions of how mathematics is taught and learned, together with the general theory and practice of instruction. Includes the empirical and theoretical methodologies specific to mathematics-education research, the study of curricula and educational systems across cultures and eras, and the cognitive and affective processes underlying mathematical learning. This block forms the meta-disciplinary scaffolding on which subject-specific pedagogy is built.

brief.name: History and society (aspects of mathematics education)
brief.definition: Studies the historical development of mathematics teaching practices and the social, cultural, and political contexts that shape mathematics education.

brief.name: Educational policy and systems
brief.definition: Examines the organization, governance, standards, and policy frameworks that structure mathematics education at institutional and national levels.

brief.name: Psychology of mathematics education, research in mathematics education
brief.definition: Investigates the cognitive, affective, and social processes involved in learning and teaching mathematics, and develops the theoretical and empirical methodologies of mathematics education research.

brief.name: Education and instruction in mathematics
brief.definition: Addresses general pedagogical theory, curriculum design, teaching methods, and instructional practice applicable across mathematical topics.

blocks.name: Education in Core Mathematical Content Areas
blocks.description: Covers the didactics of the classical pillars of the mathematics curriculum: logical and set-theoretic foundations, arithmetic and elementary number theory, geometry, algebra, and analysis. Each subclass addresses discipline-specific teaching methods, common student misconceptions, curricular sequencing, and assessment tailored to that branch of mathematics. This is the most institutionally stable and longest-studied slice of mathematics education, spanning primary-school arithmetic to university-level analysis.

brief.name: Education of foundations of mathematics
brief.definition: Concerns the teaching of logic, set theory, and the axiomatic foundations underlying mathematical reasoning.

brief.name: Education of arithmetic and number theory
brief.definition: Covers pedagogy for numeration, arithmetic operations, and elementary number-theoretic concepts, primarily in primary and early-secondary curricula.

brief.name: Geometry education
brief.definition: Studies the teaching and learning of Euclidean and other geometries, spatial reasoning, and geometric proof.

brief.name: Algebra education
brief.definition: Addresses instruction in algebraic notation, equation solving, structures, and symbolic reasoning across educational levels.

brief.name: Analysis education
brief.definition: Concerns pedagogy for calculus and mathematical analysis, including limits, continuity, differentiation, and integration.

blocks.name: Education in Quantitative, Computational, and Applied Mathematics
blocks.description: Concerns the teaching of combinatorics, graph theory, probability and statistics, mathematical modeling and applications, numerical methods, and computer science as they enter mathematics curricula. These areas have grown rapidly in curricular importance with the rise of data science, computational thinking, and STEM-integrated instruction. Pedagogical research here actively negotiates how to balance procedural computation with conceptual understanding, and instructional standards remain less settled than for the classical content areas.

brief.name: Education of combinatorics, graph theory, probability theory, and statistics
brief.definition: Covers the teaching of discrete mathematics, counting, graph structures, probabilistic reasoning, and statistical literacy.

brief.name: Education of mathematical modeling and applications of mathematics
brief.definition: Studies pedagogy for translating real-world problems into mathematical models and applying mathematics across disciplines.

brief.name: Education of numerical mathematics
brief.definition: Addresses teaching of numerical methods, approximation, and computational techniques for solving mathematical problems.

brief.name: Computer science (educational aspects)
brief.definition: Covers the pedagogy of computer science topics, programming, algorithms, and computational thinking, as taught within or alongside mathematics curricula.

blocks.name: Educational Materials, Media, and Technology in Mathematics Education
blocks.description: Studies the design, evaluation, and pedagogical use of textbooks, manipulatives, software, and digital or online platforms for teaching mathematics, including intelligent tutoring systems and computer-assisted instruction. As educational technology evolves rapidly, most recently through adaptive learning platforms and AI-based tutoring, this area sits at an active research frontier concerning efficacy, equity of access, and classroom integration. It intersects with but remains distinct from the pedagogy of computer science itself.

brief.name: Educational material and media and educational technology in mathematics education
brief.definition: Studies the design, evaluation, and classroom integration of textbooks, manipulatives, software, and digital technologies for teaching mathematics.
